Took him for Fall 2025, He's pretty good. I have basically no prior CS knowledge and he explains the content well enough where I can use it in the projects and feel confident about it later. Reviews saying he's odd are correct, he's very odd but it keeps the lecture engaging & I love him for that. All of his PDFs have passwords which is annoying at worst but literally all class procedures are written up there so I never had administrative questions because everything is explained in a "handout on ELMS." Project descriptions are detailed and lay out the guidelines in a way that even the most stupid person can follow them, which I found really helpful (lol). That said, there's basically no feedback in this class. Good lecturer for a mandatory class.

wait i love herman his lectures are so listenable to me for some reason and the mandatory attendance was annoying at first but i found it super helpful in the end. never had to cram for exams too hard and all exams are pretty fair as long as you study every slides posted. listening to the lecture also helps a ton for me, i pretty much listened to 80% of his lectures. he's not a bad option. pls let him stay above 3 rating
he's mid but good enough, explains content with depth so good if you have no prior knowledge
hes the goat but he is a bit odd but just try and youll be good

Hermann was probably the most mid professor out there. He's not bad, but not good. The class is extremely boring, and he threatens you to show up with pop quizzes, though a majority of them are in discussion, which don't have his no technology rule. he also takes forever to grade, I had maybe 4 grades in total until after the final. He did post great exam prep though, especially through the optional homework. Overall, if you don't know the content very well beforehand, he's pretty good. If you're confident in 132 content, I would avoid.

His lectures and slides were very good. The projects are extremely detailed and very useful for understanding the content. Make sure to go to every lecture and discussion because getting a 0 on a graded worksheet will heavily decrease your grade. Note that he does not allow students to use any devices during class, but this shouldn't be a big issue if you manage your time effectively outside of class.

In my opinion, Herman is a vastly underrated professor. He has three midterms and a final, and each is worth about 15-18% of your grade. He posts a 20-30 page long practice test about a week before each exam, thats filled with practice questions that are formatted and cover similar content as the corresponding exam. Basically, if you do the whole practice test and understand everything within it, you are set for the actual exam. He also posts worksheets in the form of optional homework that are good practice for exams. In terms of exam grading, he and his TA's are generally pretty generous with the grading, and I often found myself getting a higher grade than I expected. In terms of lectures, he doesnt allow electronics, which didnt really affect me as I dont use electronics in any of my classes, but he tends to be a pretty good lecturer. He covers the material at a good pace, and is very open to questions about the content. Hes also pretty funny and seems to care about his students imo. He and his TAs have plenty of office hours, and when he didnt have time to cover certain material in class, he went out of his way to make videos about it rather than direct us to the textbook to learn it ourselves, he also gave everyone three pieces of candy for halloween. He does incentivise people to come to lecture and discussion by having "surprise" graded worksheets that are cumulatively worth about 10% of the grade, so if you go to lecture and discussion, you basically get a free 10% of your grade as the worksheets are graded on completion. He also has participation points that are worth 3% of your grade where you basically have to participate a few times over the sem, so another easy 3%. As for projects, none of them were super hard, I think the longest one took me like 3 hours, he released the grades for the first two fairly quickly but waited till the end of the sem for the rest of them. He gives long instructions for the projects which have a lot of helpful details and as long as you follow that and style your code correctly you should be fine, he also gave extra credit for some of them if you finish them before the deadline. Basically, Herman is a good lecturer who cares about his students and gives ample resources and grade boosters so you can get a good grade in the class, just make sure to attend lectures and discussions and you should do fine.
I LOVE LARRY HERMAN HE IS SO BEAUTIFUL AND FUNNY AND INTELLIGENT HES THE MOST COOL PERSON IN THE WORLD YOU MUST TAKE THIS CLASS IF YOU DONT YOU WILL REGRET IT.

Herman's a decent instructor. He definitely covers all of the material you need to succeed in the class, but I've fallen asleep so many times in that class. I honestly didn't show up for most classes, and even though I did fine I would've been much better off just biting the bullet and going to class. He also didn't input grades for most of the projects until after finals, so I had no way of gauging how well I needed to do on assignments and exams. Otherwise, he does a good job at conveying everything you need to know for the class and for DSA in general. I agree with a lot of the negative points that people have brought up in the other reviews, but honestly I think his class is a hard, but fair class. Show up, read the lecture slides, do the (optional) homework, and you'll be set.

Larry Herman has a lot of quirks, and I can see why most people seem to dislike him; I, however, thought he was overall a good instructor. His lectures are well taught, and he explains concepts very well. Discussions are spent doing worksheets, which are occasionally graded for completion. He also will occasionally and randomly hand out graded worksheets during lectures--this, combined with the fact that he doesn't record lectures, means you should definitely attend as many classes as you can. His project descriptions are incredibly long and often repetitive, but this is also an advantage; you're essentially told exactly how to do each project in the description. I personally didn't find his exams to be challenging--anything he says in class or writes on the slides or handouts is fair game, and he will definitely test you on even things we spent the smallest amount of time on, but he gives a LOT of good exam prep, from the practice exams, the homeworks, and the discussion worksheets (he even admitted in lecture that he will sometimes pull exam questions from discussion worksheets, so definitely do the worksheets!). He has some negative quirks, such as not allowing electronics in class, having an interesting participation system, and being really touchy over being called a professor for some reason, but honestly the only strong negative I have for him is how incredibly long it took for everything to get graded. We had a total of 10 projects throughout this course, and by the time finals came around, we still only had the first three graded; it can be stressful, because you really don't know how well you're doing in the class if you can't know your grades until the very end. Overall: Good lecturer & exams are very passable once you understand his format, but he has a bunch of odd quirks and he's an INCREDIBLY slow grader. He's not the best instructor out there, but definitely not as bad as some of the other reviews have said. If you're forced to take his class, don't fret! Put in the work and you'll do fine. I'm giving him 4 stars because I can't give him a 3.5.

His lectures are surprisingly informative and well structured. I will say Herman is a very good lecturer. However, that’s about where the good ends. He’s insanely hard to communicate with and unforgiving in most aspects. His practice questions make little sense(and answer keys often have wrong, conflicting or no information) and his projects are insanely strange with random requirements left and right and big deductions. All grades including projects are entered last minute with no indication of when they’ll be done. My experience was overall negative. While he will teach you very well, most, if not all of the administrative work he does is very confusing or unsatisfactory. Take his class if you genuinely want to learn, but understand that you likely will have to deal with most things above.

Absolutely terrible, if you want a stress free life, don't take her as your professor. Her lectures don't teach you anything at all; she often skips over thins in class and unexpectedly puts them in exams. She doesn't take her class seriously at all, and instead just giggles around most of the time. You will hear a lot about how she was part of an IT company long back, and very little about the course content at all. If you like self teaching content, then take this class. P.S. You'll find that only 7 people end up attending lecture regularly at the end of the day, because her classes are absolutely useless at the end of the day.

I took Elias for 131 and 132 and I will say that he is definitely the best introductory CS professor anyone could ask for. I was very scared coming into UMD because I had pretty much no true coding experience (I had not even taken AP CS principles) and I had heard from various planetterp reviews and forums that this course would be difficult if I did not already have at least some background in CS. However, Elias made both of these courses a great experience. He truly cares about his students and takes time to talk about topics such as mental health. He also has a system set up so that there are nearly constant office hours making it very easy to get help with projects or leading up to exams. His lectures were also very engaging. He explained concepts very well (especially for someone with no prior knowledge) and was always very receptive to questions (as I used to ask a lot especially in 132), making sure his answers contained as much depth as possible. In addition to being a great teacher and having a system that makes it easy to get help, he also records lectures, provides a plethora of practice material for midterms and the final, and overall is just a very approachable person. Would definitely recommend.

Mr. Herman (not professor, don't call him that he'll go on a tangent about addressing him incorrectly) is a pretty good professor. I think he gets a lot of hate because you aren't allowed to use any electronic devices during lecture, but he does it so you can actually focus in class. Some lectures can be quite boring if you kind of of already know what he's talking about, but I felt he covered the course material really well. His lecture slides contained a lot of the content that you needed to know for the class, so reviewing them before every exam was very helpful. He also provides example code that help you understand how concepts work if you are unable to understand it immediately. He does waste the beginning of class for reminders that no one really needs to hear. Exams were very fair. He gave practice material that reflected exactly what was on the exam. As long as you study hard for the exams, it should be pretty easy to get an A. No lecture recordings, and you have to attend lecture and discussion every time because he will randomly have graded worksheets. They are 11% of your grade but is a guaranteed 100 as long as you show up. 3% is class participation, so just raise your hand 3 times during the semester. Overall, a very solid professor. If he is the only option you have, there's nothing to worry about. You will still learn everything you need to know, just accustom yourself to how he runs the class.

CS instructor (not professor, as he made very clear), yet no electronics are allowed any time in class. Not afraid to single people out if you leave lecture early. Assigned an exam immediately after fall break and immediately after thanksgiving break, so be prepared to study during all your breaks! Projects aren't so bad, but exams are pretty hard. Optional homework (but you should probably do them they actually are helpful). Graded on participation which is kind of weird for a lecture of 200+ people. All of his lecture slides are posted on Canvas as files so you have to individually download each one to view them, same goes for homework and projects, which was kind of annoying. Herman also doesn't answer his email and specifically asks you not to email him so the only opportunity you'll have to ask questions is office hours. Overall it isn't terrible, but I'd say try for a different professor if you can.

tl;dr Good lecturer for the most part, but class is boring and no technology. Midterms are fair for the most part but finals were a bit harder. Larry Herman is a good "instructor" in that he can definitely teach the topics. Has a weird obsession with small mammals for some reason, and makes it his goal to incorporate them into every possible problem. Scattered in-lecture graded worksheets forced you to show up, but he just stopped giving them suddenly after being behind schedule. No technology during class was lwky annoying, but you can compensate with napping or quietly talking with friends. He provides good review material and practice tests that prepare you decently. For all exams, there are usually a combination of MCQs, code output, writing code, and misc. MCQs were definitely something to note, as you could pick multiple answers and they're always on some niche topic (you lose more points for not circling a correct answer than circling an incorrect answer). Overall, as long as you show up to discussions and lecturers, you should do relatively well in the class. Would not take again tho.

1. No technology in class. The classes where you already know the content and can dedicate your time to LITERALLY anything else are rendered useless by this rule. It's honestly just annoying cuz why can't we as college students decide for ourselves what method of notetaking works best for you. 2. Wasted time during lecture. Oh my god. This was what locked me out the most. I swear, the whole first month of semester, he'd go over class rules like the tech rule and the stupid rule where you have to sit in the closest seat available to the front. Like what are we doing. He would straight up stop talking during his already incomprehensible lecture where he's always like UHHHHH AHHHH UGHHHHHH *STUTTERSTUTTER* to tell students to sit closer. 3. Test format. The MCQs are absolutely horrible. All his MCQ are multiple select and if you choose a wrong one you get points subtracted. Not to mention the MCQs are often about the most obscure stuff and have cost me multiple letter grades. 4. Bro he has all of his pdfs that access the project documents and homeworks as password protected, so you can never access them in Canvas directly and always have to download them. This may not seem like an issue reading this now, but wait till your trying to study or cram. It is so annoying. 5. Random ahh graded worksheets that make you come to lecture. Since many would not come to lecture if there wasn't something else driving them, herman has surprise graded worksheets in both discussion and lecture. Towards the end of the semester, I didn't really care anymore and decided to take my chances, and it worked out, but it definitely wasted a lot of my time just waiting in lecture to maybe get a worksheet. If you're able to get past the constant interruptions and yapping during lecture, I guess I can say he does teach the content, but don't all professors? Maybe not.. Everything that's on the tests he'll have talked about before hand but again, it can be obscure stuff sometimes.

Solid lecturer but... Classes can be extremely boring, depending on the topic projects are extremely tedious, but VERY helpful for understanding the content worksheets force you to attend discussions (or just find a friend in an earlier one) Exams are fair except for multiple-choice questions, as their design is quite unfair and leads to you second-guessing yourself doesn't let you call him professor (this is kinda funny, I'll admit) All documents in Canvas are locked behind a password that is only accessible by downloading the document (on desktop) Final was tough, and getting a 100 is quite hard due to the aforementioned mcqs slow grader (most projects still haven't been graded) will yap A LOT TLDR: if you want to learn, take Herman, if you want an A, take Nora/whoever else is teaching

Good for learning if you can manage to pay attention to his boring lectures. Very annoying policy with technology and will call you out if he sees it. Exams are fair if you are really paying attention, the mcq sections are annoying though. The weird locked pdfs is super annoying and there for seemingly no reason. If you pay attention and focus on learning everything you will be fine.

90% of the hate she receives is honestly completely unjustified. Not only were the exams mostly fair (aside from some weird 2 point MCQS), she covered all the content thoroughly in class with projects as supplementation. When people say that she is a bad professor, I feel like they are the students that never show up to lecture, chatgpt the projects, and are then surprised that the exam isn't a cake walk. Yes, the exams were more difficult than I expected and did need a decent amount of preparation or you would run out of time trying to solve the FRQS during the test.

NORA BURKHAUSER: A generous lecturer with an energetic attitude at 9 AM. Is willing to help you learn the concepts and review for the final, and prioritizes student well-being. We even got CMSC 132 stickers :) LECTURES (Rating: 3/5): The PowerPoints were somewhat helpful in teaching us the conceptual stuff behind OOP, as that is what is mostly covered on the exam, and the review for the final was okay. It would’ve been nice to slow down or go over things more in depth, as several topics were rushed. But I do like that the lectures are recorded (especially for a 9 AM lecture), so missing one isn’t the end of the world! DISCUSSIONS (Rating: 5/5): Discussions depend on your TA. My TA was chill and went over important concepts that are needed for the projects and exams. We also had review sessions in discussion before lecture tests. HOMEWORK (Rating: 5/5): The homework assignments in this class are the programming projects. When I took 132 there were nine. If you put the time into them and learn how to implement what is needed you will succeed. One of the projects involves you working with a partner. I had a wonderful partner for that project. QUIZZES AND LABS (Rating: 4/5): The labs are free grades, so I can’t complain. You just need to try. For quizzes, Prof. B. allows you to bring a sheet of notes into the quiz - not only does this help you study but can also give you some help if you’re stuck. Some quizzes were tricky. EXAMS (Rating: 2/5): They’re okay, but they need to be shorter or there needs to be more time (50 minutes isn’t enough). I also find it weird how you handwrite code in a computer science class when in reality you type code. Do mock exams in your dorms (lucky you if you have them) to make sure you master the pacing of the questions. OVERALL RATING: 3.8/5 Structure for Fall 2025: - Programming Projects (26%; distributed across nine) - Quizzes (2.5% each; top four are graded with six given) - Labs (3%; graded for completion, top 3 graded with seven given) - Exams (12%, 14%, and 15% respectively) - Final Exam (20%)

Larry is the type of instructor that you absolutely hate if you're failing the class, but if you're doing well then you really appreciate everything he does. I personally just pay attention in class and he goes over all the exam material very thoroughly, and as much as I hated the no tech thing initially, it is really helpful for preventing distractions and cultivating the good habit of not being dependent on your phone 247. Basically as long as you listen in class and do his practice questions then you can do well on the exams. As for taking off points for wrong answers, that's only for one section of the exam and it's generally pretty easy. The only reason he does that is because they're all multi-select and people could just circle every single answer if there was no penalty for selecting the incorrect ones. The only annoying thing about Herman is the projects. The grade is dependent on a lot of stuff like secret tests and style, which you can't know how you scored until you get the grade back. The grading TAs are always way behind so you essentially don't know if you're doing anything wrong the whole semester and you could keep getting stuff wrong due to lack of feedback. Like right now I have an A but if I somehow messed up all my projects that haven't been returned yet then I could end up with a B for sure

I think that Herman's class isn't as bad as people say it is. He has a weird thing about people calling him professor, he doesn't let you use any sort of technology in class, he's kind of an odd guy and a pretty boring lecturer (but he can be funny sometimes). He does have really long project descriptions, but they're pretty helpful in that there's no confusion about what you have to do for the projects and what coding is required. It's honestly more of a blessing than a curse. His tests are fair, and he gives you lengthy practice exams before every test that are formatted the same as the actual exams. Some questions on the exams end up being pretty similar to what was given on practice exams, but that isn't always the case. Attendance is technically not mandatory, but he gives random graded worksheets during lectures and discussions that are in total worth ~10% of your grade (the worksheets themselves are graded on completion so you really only have to do 3/4 of it to get a 100, plus the worksheet questions are similar to exam coding questions) so you kinda have to go. He doesn't record lectures because bro doesn't know how to use a computer, but he does post lecture slides. He also posts homework questions that you don't have to do, but they can help with exam practice. Overall he's a pretty mid professor that makes his project descriptions and handouts wayyyy too long and yaps too much, but the class itself is easy enough.

Overall, I feel that Larry is a pretty deccent professor. He certainly has some unconventional rules, but they are to benefit you as a student. I felt the course was very structured, but in the end not structured incredibly well. We spent a lot of time on certain topics that weren't that important, and ended up having to rush the end of the semester. He also spends about 10 minutes at the beginning of every lecture going the same stuff about rules and reminders. But in terms of learning, he does a pretty decent job. He explains topics well, and provides very good material to prepare for exams. I wouldn't see he is a fan favorite, but he is still a good professor.
Assigning a project over thanksgiving break wtf herman :(

While she is a nice teacher and is mindful of her students, she teaches this class pretty poorly. The slides look very generic and are just ran through. She also tends to mess up on sample code, and half of the time I don't even understand what is supposed to be taught. I learned more from just looking at the slides myself and asking ChatGPT (please don't use ChatGPT on projects, only as a studying tool) to teach me the topics than from the few times I've actually gone to lecture and sat in class. The exams are easy as long as you are actively learning rather than just dilly dallying and depending on lectures. Either way, this course is easy and basically more introductory Computer Science so professor quality doesn't matter as much.

Fawzi made CMSC132 a very fair and easy class. Exams, projects, and quizzes were all fair in my opinion. He never really asked trick questions, or if he did, he made a point to emphasize their answers during lectures. He did an overall good job of explaining concepts in class. I would recommend taking him for CMSC132 .

I think Mr. Larry gets too much hate for not letting students use your phone in class, and he will call you out if he sees you. However he is a very good lecturer and I found his class to be difficult but rewarding and I think he prepares you much better for later courses than other professors for 132.
I fear I was too harsh on my last review. Honestly all of the "bad" policies are good for the students and for learning, just a bit of an annoying class but so is every other CS class.

This guy is a weird professor, what I have to say about him is that the projects he gives have very in depth explanations on what they want so sometimes they are good, but sometimes they are just inherently hard as well. However, he does have some weird policies. First off he allows 0 electronic devices during his lectures, now if it was just a ban on phones, that's completely fine and I would understand that. However, he also has a ban on any kind of electronic device for note taking, so if you purely take notes on an ipad or a laptop, be ready to buy a notebook. To add to that, if he sees any kind of phone or anything being used in class, he will immediately stop whatever he is saying and single that person out and tell them to put away the phone. Which ends up wasting around 5 minutes every lecture for this. Another thing about him is that his lectures are incredibly boring, just an incredibly monotone voice that is almost inviting and pulling you to zone out during his class, sometimes he breaks the monotone-ness of his lecture by making a lil joke. But those are few and far between so don't count on it. Also for the first 10 minutes of every class he has a page with announcements which is partially fine, mostly just to tell people when grades are out and when things are due. What is quite annoying is that he will constantly circle the top of it while saying "Students with accommodations please see me in office hours" and every lecture he says it, it slowly gets more and more condescending. What I mean by this is that he starts to say "Look here. Look here. Can youg guys see what this says" in a condescending tone As well as this, he has a weird feud with emailing, as he will get incredibly mad if you try to email him about something instead of seeing him in office hours. Also for some reason he has a weird thing with students not calling him professor? Not too sure what that's about. Overall: 1.5/5

I feel like Professor Burkhauser's reviews were a little bit low during her first semester teaching. However, I don't think that Prof. B is a bad teacher. She has gotten better with her teaching over time and is very friendly to the students. Less mistakes were made during her lectures, and she was very knowledegable getting the concepts of CMSC 132 across. If you have a choice, take her. Your grade will thank you, and you will learn quite a lot.
Doesn't allow laptops and notes, canvas page is extremely hard to navigate, grating voice, randomly brash and rude to random students during lectures, avoid if possible

Literally had a nightmare a semester after taking a class with this guy about being back in one of his exams. Not a fan of how he doesn't allow electronics, and he's also a stickler for calling people out for being on them. His projects are also insanely descriptive.

Practice exams are completely useless, same as the lecture. Homework projects will not help you anything on the actual exam. Quiz is hard as possible, I don't know why. Leet code and any outside resources may help you survive from the hell on earth he created. Take Elias or Nora. Curves are +1 point on your final grade, haha.

elias my goat !!!!! tl;dr if you have the option to take him, do yourself the favor and do so (be it for 131, 132, etc) there's not much more to be said regarding the actual structure of his class (recorded lectures, good projects, straightforward + thorough project descriptions, open note quizzes, considerate regarding extensions) - it's really well organized as you can probably tell, j want to add he really is as chill of a dude as is written below lol - cracks jokes in lecture, super nice abt considering + answering questions, laid back office hours, j super personable in general (on top of being a fantastic educator). class was certainly challenging throughout, but i think it was for good reasons (tough concepts, problems that tested my understanding of certain topics, etc), rather than contrived requirements or vague expectations. i definitely came out starting to feel like a computer scientist (+ a stronger programmer in general), and would absolutely do it over again given the chance.

Pros: Records lectures (each and every lecture and lab/disscussion was recorded and posted on time with clear audio and no issues!) Is self aware enough to adjust difficulty. (First 2 exams had low averages - like 60s - so he made the 3rd exam easier and gave open note quizzes that we did outside of class. I believe he also towards the end made projects weigh more which was appreciated. Gives 2 minute breaks in the middle of lectures. Is active on piazza and gives clarifications Explains concepts mostly clearly and draws things out which is helpful. Gives exams details on piazza as hints which do help on exams. Curves? This is kind of a pro and a con because he doesn’t curve anything (not even exams) and instead moves the grade cutoffs at the end of the semester. This is a good way of doing it but unfortunately he only moved them by 1 point so while many were grateful some were a bit frustrated as the exam averages were pretty low this semester. Cons: Hard exams due to lack of practice material (old exams are useless since he changed the curriculum). Also the instructions can at times be confusing and he limits what you can use (like what data structures and loops etc) which doesn’t work well with the instructions. I do think he did a better job with this on the final but for the first 3 exams it was an issue. Discussions/labs are basically mini lectures instead of actual practice. The topics form lab do show up on exams but usually only in short answer. His voice is slightly monotone which can be hard for some people. TLDR: He’s a decent prof and if you put in the effort you won’t fail but taking Elias or maybe Nora might be better.

2.5⭐ (rounded to 3 stars) Professor Burkhauser is a kind and understanding individual, but is relatively new to the Java language and teaching at a college level. She speaks in a rather dry tone, and her lectures are generally difficult to comprehend. She also cannot always answer questions properly. Overall, everything can be taught in a much simpler manner, and you have to teach yourself the content. Another aspect to note is that she often makes mistakes, and this is present in almost every exam. Moreover, the exam and quiz questions and format are unpredictable. Having said all of this, I do respect and appreciate her for reflecting on student grades and giving curves. She also makes applications to the real world at times, which are interesting. I do not have an entirely pleasant experience in discussion, either, since the quizzes are graded harshly, and my TA wants to get started immediately and finish as early as possible, rather than focusing on going through every problem step by step and making sure everyone understands each step. What I do like, though, is that we, students, can work with each other on problems and create an interactive environment. Office hours are pretty helpful, depending on who you work with, especially for the more difficult projects. The projects tend to be quite long and complex, so my suggestion is to always begin early. Some projects have early deadlines in addition to the normal deadlines, allowing you to earn bonus points, which is nice.
It's ironic how he is a CS professor and yet he bans the use of computers in class. Just don't take class with him and your life will be all hunkydory

Pros: Data structure lectures are clear Projects are interesting: 7 of the 8 projects focused understanding, then implementing homemade versions of data structures, as opposed to niche java concepts Gives 2 minute breaks in the middle of every lecture Cons: Exams are incredibly difficult and more specifically, time crunched. They mostly focus on problem solving as opposed to java concepts (more creativity, less memorization, which I prefer, but is much more challenging). The core problem of Pedram’s exams are not the questions he asked, but the lack of time. Questions typically ask you to develop an algorithm to solve a given problem. None of the practice exams for any of the midterms or the final reflected what was on the exam whatsoever. Exams this semester asked you a number of coding questions, all of which require you to develop an algorithm and solve a problem on the fly. Previous exams do not ask these sort of things, and are thus useless. He said that past exams not being reflective of current ones is INTENTIONAL. Other reviews have stated that students failing is a “skill issue”. However as someone who received an A, I disagree. If large swathes of students are doing poorly (as indicated by the aggregate data (mean/medians on exam 1 and exam 2)), the exams may not be entirely fair (although Exam 1 scores may be a result of poor preparation and inaccurate practice exams. Pedram reformatted his exams this semester). Discussion is effectively a second lecture where TA’s cover additional topics not taught by Pedram in lecture. Topics covered by Pedram in lecture are not reinforced. They did let us out 20 minutes early though. Talks kind of quickly during lecture, making it harder to digest material. Other: Individual assignments, such as exams, were not curved. However, Pedram did say he curved our final grades, but he did not say by how much. A 91.59 was considered an A Pedram is a bit monotime, but it did not bother me much. If you are looking for an easier class, Ellias may be better for your GPA (I had him for 131), but for a more interesting/instructive class that prepares you better for interviews, I would take Pedram. Grade Statistics (you can plug into a normal distribution if you want): Exam 1: Mean: 62.77 , Median: 65.0, Standard Deviation: 21.04 Exam 2: Mean : 64.82, Median: 66.0, Standard Deviation: 15.04 Exam 3: Mean: 76.66, Median: 78.0, Standard Deviation 14.27 Final Exam: Mean: 144.16 (out of 200), Standard Deviation: 30.67 Final Overall Grade: Mean: 81.10, Standard Deviation: 9.35

I second that the exam averages for all the midterms were low (around 60s) but to me it didn't really affect me. At the end he did a curve by changing the grade ranges for letter grades and gave as a quite generous detailed outline of the content on the final which was really helpful. It did seem towards the end he was doing a lot of help to boost everyone's low averages although I think it would've been better for this to be something focused on throughout the semester instead of the last weeks. For the content of the class, it is not too complicated but there will be a lot of memorization (which I personally dislike). Like memorization of the time complexities and just a bunch of terms/definitions. Great professor when it comes to the way the content is taught, more oriented to learning than just to passing the class.

I ended with a 91% and got an A so honestly I'm happy. In the middle of the semester, I was gonna give him a lower rating but honestly my performance on the 2nd exam was completely my fault. I kinda hated some of the projects though bc they were hard to figure out. He talks slow and isn't that engaging in class. So if u decide to skip lecture, watch his recorded lecture videos!!!!! DONT GET BEHIND!!! That was my main problem I just let the lecture and discussion videos pile up and did so bad on the 2nd exam bc I watched 3 weeks of lecture in the week leading up to the exam. His teaching isn't bad just search up extra help or explanations if u need. Also discussion sucks I only went to the first week lol I also had him for CMSC131 and gave him 5 stars as well. Maybe it's because I ended up doing really well but he's not terrible. Use all the resources u can: TAs and office hours, online videos, ask AI for practice problems, etc idk
A course that has no curve with consistent 60 averages on exams lol. Teaches you how to drive a car and expects you to know how to build it.

Curve used for final grading seemed to only benefit a small majority of the class. It also didn't help that this semester, he was re-arranging the entire curriculum, meaning past practice exams weren't all that helpful.

There is no curve. English 101 and this course will have the exact same grading scale. Good luck. Pedram is a very nice guy and I have absolutely nothing against him it's just kind of insane that our grade averages were barely 60's and other professors had 88's consistently, along with no curve for the final grade. good luck
Hard class with no curve. English 101 and this course will have the same grading breakdown. Take that for what you will

I’ll try to summarize Pedram’s classes as best as I can: the lectures are extremely monotonous, and he’s not the most engaging lecture. However, his explanations are fantastic, especially when watching back the lecture videos at 2x speed. I know he somewhat revamped the curriculum this semester, but he did stay organized pretty well. It is pretty annoying that he doesn’t use Canvas, but it’s manageable. In regards to exams, which is everybody’s big beef with the way he teaches this class, they are for sure harder than any of the other professors who teach this class, and the projects are also more difficult. I will give him credit though for realizing this midway through the semester, adapting, and giving us in-depth outlines about what to expect on exams. I also liked the quiz format he used for the final two quizzes, even though I’m sure he put them that way because he ran out of time. I feel like I learned quite a bit in this class, and I was lucky to finish with an A, but if I had to go back and reschedule I would take it with someone else.

I had Pedram for both 131 and 132 and he did a good job for both. He is very organized, explains things clearly and focused, records his lectures, and his slides are generally very helpful. Also there are no surprises with assignments as he gives a heads up about every assignment. His projects and exams are definitely on the challenging side but I think they are overall pretty fair and you just need to be able to think logically/conceptually for the exams and rewatch lecture videos for the projects. Pedram was also very accommodating when he saw a lot of people didn't do well on an exam so he boosted the weight of the projects and also made 2 quizzes asynchronous easy 100's that you could complete in 10 minutes. Also he made the last midterm and the final lowkey a breeze. The TAs are pretty good too and they make Kahoots before every exam which are excellent review and usually have some good hints for what is on the exam. I have definitely seen that Pedram has become less monotonous than he was last semester in CMSC131 . Although still not the most entertaining lecturer, I can see his spirit and personality coming out a bit, and he keeps his lectures focused and understandable. I think the reason why people don't do well in this class and complain is because they don't commit enough time to truly learn the concepts, don't go to lecture and then catch up on all the videos right before the exam, and also realize that he is testing your ability to apply the concepts instead of just asking you straight up what the concepts are. My advice is to watch the lecture videos a few times if you don't understand something, and also Youtube videos about the algorithms were so helpful. Overall, I have no major complaints about Pedram and I would definitely recommend him if you're willing to truly learn and do work.

I took Fawzi for CMSC132 in fall 2023 and it is still to this day one of my favorite classes I have ever taken at UMD. This is the gold standard for a CS class at UMD. His lectures were very engaging and he explained things super clearly (despite teaching a bit quickly sometimes). Discussion exercises really helped reinforce the material and the discussions quizzes were very fair. Not to mention his exams were also super fair with high medians. Projects as well were very interesting (especially the Blackjack game, minimum snippet, and graph algorithms maze gui). Final exam was also super fair. Just in general, this class was very well structured. TAs were also top-notch (shoutout to Aram, Rebecca, Day, and Amelia). I really loved this class and I hope he teaches it again in the future as he is mainly teaching CMSC250 now. Overall Rating: 5/5

Her lectures aren't perfect like others have mentioned, but her slides and code examples are really good for learning the content. She has weekly projects, which if you're interested in computer science are somewhat enjoyable and also teach the content quite well (if you do well on the projects, you can do well on quizzes). I would recommend her and I think she deserves higher than her current reviews suggest.

Most of these comments are hate tbh... He really does prepare you well for the exam, it's their fault, mine included, that we didn't study according to his guide. The second exam that had such a low average was only so low cause no one did the project first as he recommended. I noticed after having done his project that the exam would have been much easier. I went through most of my exams, and maybe these dudes are genuinely having a more difficult time than I am, but most of my mistakes were trivial. I wouldn't attribute that to him being a problem though. I don't think he's perfect, but if I could, I'd give a 4.5. Rounding down due to certain things that are put in place that I just don't agree with (Style options for example)

The previous reviews are pretty accurate. Pedram isn't the most interesting professor and his voice can lull you to sleep. However, the main problem I have with him are his exams. His averages were WAYY lower than those of Elias and Nora. The previous exams he provided were far easier than the exams he actually gave us this semester. On Exam 1, the only code-writing on the practice exams was doing basic things with class definition. But on the actual midterm were really strange algorithms that were difficult to understand. He doesn't really do a good job of preparing you for his midterms. His projects were also extremely boring, as every single one of them was remaking a data structure from scratch rather than using it in a cool way. Everyone took Pedram in Spring 2025 because Elias was full and Nora had really bad reviews, so everyone saw him as the 2nd best. Just do yourself a favor and take one of the other professors unless you want your grade to suffer.
interesting dude, decent lectures. very structured and organized projects.

Terrible prof, very nice lady She isn't a terrible person or anything but this class is very disorganized and she isn't great at engaging her students. I fell asleep first day of lecture. I'm sure the content would be manageable if it was properly taught but I pretty much taught myself this entire course. If you are technically inclined or very interested in programming I am sure you would do great but if you actually want to understand the topic, I'd go with Elias (if she is co teaching with Elias you might as well go to his lecture's cause everything else is his anyways).

Monotonous lecturer but still gets the point across. His exam averages are extremely low, which shouldn't be a problem if every other 132 professor has exam averages that low. But that isn't the case; for the first two exams of the Spring 2025 semester, we had averages in the mid to low 60s. The third exam had an average in the mid 70s. This wouldn't be a problem if 132 were a weed-out class, but it seems like only Pedram treats it like one. At the end of the day, he isn't a terrible professor but his exams are unnecessarily hard.

Insanely boring professor with the strictest rules. No missing class whatsoever, unless you have a university approved reason, and even if you submit an absence request he won't respond for weeks on end until finally denying you weeks after the request was made. No electronics in class, including ipads. Not a problem, just buy a notebook, but just seems so unnecessary. Reminds you to call him Larry in every lecture and gets weirdly upset if someone refers to him as a professor. Only bright part is that he occasionally makes jokes about frogs and other animals but the fact that he docks points from your grade for the craziest things- accidentally writing down the wrong discussion time on a midterm, forgetting to write your name on the odd numbered pages- takes away from any sort of friendly jokes he might make.

The lectures are educational, but don't do much to actually prepare you for quizzes or exams. Do leetcode or Hacker rank problems they will prepare you for exams and quizzes much better. As one of his guarantees are that exams and quizzes are things you have never done before. Pedram knows what he is talking about, but going to the lectures will only give you a basic idea of what is happening, no applications are explained at all just how data structures are put together on a High level. Other profs have average exam scores in the 80's while our first 2 were around 65 with exam 3's being high at 77.
Even though you can still learn from his monotonous teaching style, be prepared for a potential drop in your GPA or the risk of not meeting your benchmark requirements.

Great person, not a great lecturer. She wants the best for her students but her teaching can be very misleading, so only take her if she's co-teaching with a professor like Elias so that you can watch his lecture videos.

I dont get the hate. He is a pretty good professor and definitely knows what he teaches. He also gives 2 minute breaks every lecture which I am a big fan of. I understand data structures and algorithms well thanks to professor Sadeghian. One complaint I have is that the test are very difficult but if you understand the concepts that you should not be doing terrible on the exams.
Not a great lecturer but she's really nice and if you're ready to self study you'll do fine (if she co-teaches with Elias).

DO NOT TAKE PEDRAM!!! HE IS ONE OF THE WORST PROFESSORS OUT THERE. HIS EXAMS ARE SO HARD!!! THE ONLY REASON HIS REVIEWS ARE DECENT BEFORE IS BECAUSE HE WAS CO-TEACHING WITH NELSON. HE BY HIMSELF IS STRAIGHT TRASHHH!!!!!!!!!!! RUNNNNNN AWAYYYYY!!!!!!

Took him for 131 and 132, and I don't know what else to say that hasn't already been said. He will do everything he can to let you succeed in this class, and doesn't believe in the philosophy of weed-out classes. He's very lenient on project deadlines, especially if you communicate with him. His exams are also very similar to review and lecture material, and there are rarely trick questions or content that has never shown up before - the high exam averages reflect this. He is happy to reiterate topics and answer questions, and is just a really great guy overall. Please take him if he's available, the other CS professors here don't come close. Still, it's only fair to mention some of his downsides as well. Because he's so silly, he sometimes gets sidetracked and starts talking about some random lore, and the day after exams, spends 15 minutes reviewing extra credit drawings from the previous exam. This makes the class much more enjoyable, but if you're purely looking for the highest density of information, Elias tends to be a little bit slower.
He makes this class much harder than it needs to be. Bad at teaching, very boring, and exams are extremely difficult. DONT TAKE HIM !

DO NOT take this professor. Only reason of giving him two starts is that he records his lectures and that they are a good source of understanding data structures. His projects doesn't make sense at all. His projects are basically students making a clone of data structures rather then using data structures to do something creative. So when he asks you to code something using data structure for the exam most students have no idea what to do. He requires insane amounts of students tests for some projects. No late tokens are given for any of the projects. Elias and Nora on the other hand had fun projects including some group projects unlike Pedram. Pedram's graded assignments are annoyingly hard. After the first quiz and first 2 exams he kind of realized that his graded assignments were to difficult for students so he made other quizzes and the last exam a little easier. First two exam averages were a D. Third exam wasn't that bad. A lot of students with good CMSC131 grades took Pedram because Nora seemed bad as she only have reviews from 1 semester but trust me you are better off with her because grades of most student who took Pedram thinking he is better than Nora are suffering. Take Nora(or Elias) to save yourself.

Herman gets a lot of people thinking he's terrible because of his teaching style but in reality it helps a lot. It is kinda crazy to think that he bans all electronic devices in a Computer Science class but it basically gives you no choice but to focus. His lectures are definitely boring but he truly gives you everything you need to succeed. The classworks are easy points just for trying, the projects were somewhat tedious but very doable within a day or two. The exams were also not too bad, the study guides are super detailed and help a lot. Overall if you just pay attention for most of the class (I spent a lot of time drawing) an A is not hard at all.

Angie is one of the best TAs I've ever had. She goes so far above and beyond what is asked of her, it's insane. She puts in an enormous amount of work into her discussion section, I'd say more than some professors put into their entire sections. She explains everything super clearly and even gives you the "big picture" of concepts we learn. She also talks to us like her friends rather than students, and talks to us about how we're feeling about the class and how we're doing, rather than just us sitting down and being talked at. Would 100% gladly have Angie as my TA again; she is AWESOME.

She is Pedram's lead and teaching TA for CMSC131 and CMSC132 . She wasn't even my TA for either class BUT she's literally the real goat. I always watch her lab/discussion videos because they're the best ones. Also she's amazing for review and project help during office hours.

Herman was a pretty meh lecturer, his lectures were a bit monotonous and hard to sit through because he doesn't allow electronics and his voice is a bit droning. However, I wouldn't say that he is a horrible professor, just nothing special. He is better than the average professor because he knows what he's talking about unlike others cough cough Burkhauser cough and his exams really aren't that hard if you know what you're doing and just practice. His projects are not long either and very reasonable as long as you're responsible. I would say that the wild card with him is his TAs both in grading and teaching. If you get a bad teaching TA, you're definitely going to have a tougher time but that too is manageable. However, the grades on projects really depend on who's your grading TA, which can be a nightmare. I don't know what's up with some of them and their god complexes as if they weren't in the same position a couple of years ago. But those are like a coin flip and there are some really good TAs there as well. Honestly, as long as you go to lectures, take notes, and pay attention he really isn't that bad and he has his funny moments. It's definitely not the type of course you can skip and cruise through if you want an A but it's not unreasonable.

I took Nora's 132 class last semester, and it's safe to say it was an absolute mess. I can only write this review now as I have calmed down enough to finally think about her class without wanting to pull out my hair. Her class was like watching someone try to bake a cake with no recipe and none of the right ingredients, and also painfully slowly. She spent half of the class doing coding examples, but had no clue how to even do them. When people asked questions, she couldn't even answer and would spend the next 5 minutes rambling without even coming CLOSE to the question. I have to give her credit for her slideshows, though, because they weren't actually that bad. HOWEVER. Her projects were completely done by the TAs because AGAIN she has no clue what she's doing. Her exams were nothing like the practice exams, and she would laugh and joke about making them too long. And they were too long. She doesn't know how to even write an exam properly. There would also always be a mistake in the exam. Like, be so for real. I had to teach myself EVERYTHING (forever in debt to brocode my goat fr 🙏). I think part of the reason why she was so terrible was because it was her first semester teaching, so I pray to GOD that she has gotten better. If you have to take her, I'll keep you in my prayers.

Do NOT take this professor! All the reviews are outdated, probably from when he was coteaching with a better professor. The averages for his midterms are consistently in the low 60s, and his teaching is dry and mediocre at best. There are barely any resources to prepare for an exam, as the past exams are nothing like the ones he gives you. He has literally said "Every semester, students tell me that the exam felt harder and different from past exams. The fact that the exams are different is intentional", and, "If I had taken this as my second CS class, I too would have found it difficult". If you want your GPA to suffer, take this professor. Otherwise, try your best to take Elias or Nora instead.
exams were extremely difficult, not the best at teaching either. Don't recommend at all!

Do not take this class. Take Elias or Nora (Elias is co-teaching with her) at all costs. The other reviews are outdated and don't accurately put into terms how hard, unfair, and strict this class is when compared to Elias. I can only depict my indignancy in this post and hope others do not make the same mistake I did by assuming Nora is bad because of her previous ratings.

I regret taking Pedram over Nora. His tests are so much harder than other professors. The average on the first midterm was a D when for other professors, their midterms have B averages. Directions on tests and projects can be so annoying as they are sometimes these huge convoluted paragraphs. This is especially annoying for tests because you spend so much time deciphering what Pedram wants instead of doing the questions. Projects can be pretty annoying since you'll be following all of the rules given to you but then some random piazza post suddenly reveals some rule that you were never aware of, causing you to have to redo entire parts of a project. He also for some reason decided to restructure how he teaches the class so there are some topics where he just doesn't teach and leaves to his TA's. This can suck if you don't have a TA who explains things well. Avoid Pedram is you don't want your grade to suffer.

Idk why anyone is giving her above a 1 or 2. Dont get me wrong she is a nice ol lady who will grace you with somewhat leniency. She is just an ABSOLUTE terrible professor. I CANNOT STAND going to her class. she talks SOOO slow and I end up falling asleep every class. She takes SOOO long to teach such SIMPLE topics and doesn't even teach them correctly. She messes up on the SMALLEST and SIMPLEST mistakes and the STUDENTS be telling her WHAT SHE DID WRONG. I genuinely want to meet the person who hired her as a 132 LECTURER. She reads off another teacher's slides and gives another teacher's project and EXAM. SMH nora SMH. Talk about lazy. Dont even BOTHER going to class. hell you don't even have to watch her recording. I watch like 1 or 2 youtube videos on whatever topic she is teaching and I understand MORE from that than her lecture recordings. she ALWAYS talking about her old jobs and always on sum random bs that NO ONE GAF ABOUT. AND DONT EVEN GET ME STARTED ON THE BS SHE PULLED IN THE FIRST EXAM. This mf literally made a mistake in the FIRST question (had 7mc on it) and DID NOT tell us until like 10-15 mins in. Whole class groans and ALL she says is "Oopsies!" I HATE this class so much. but u know who i hate MORE? NORA MF BURKHAUSER
Avoid at all costs. Unnecessarily hard weed out class.

His exams are way harder compared to other professors and completely unnecessary. He teaches concepts in lecture but the things he asks his students to apply the concepts on are on a completely new level.

SOS!! DO NOT REDEEEEEEM!! IF PEDRAM'S CLASS WAS DIFFICULT, THIS IS WORSE! Guys anyone who gave her more than a 1 has either already learned or has sufficient knowledge to pass the class. She cannot teach at all. Every day MUST have a 5-minute ramble about how she used to work in IT. When someone asks a question she's always like: "Hmmm. You know that's a good question. Let me think about it." and then never thinks about it. What she teaches and says is a gamble, 50/50 it's either right or wrong.

I have had Nora for both semesters. While many rated her teaching in the fall as abysmal, she has noticeably improved. She is more straightforward with assignments, does not make as many errors during lectures, and is more prominent with communication. The workload is much more balanced than before, and tests are not stringent if you keep up with readings and assignments. Will love to see what she has to offer in the future!

Truly one of the greatest lecturers I have had in my entire educational career. He made the curriculum come alive in a way no other teacher ever has for me. There were days I would love to just keep having him lecture. However, he is not necessarily the best as an overall professor due to his lack of office hours and outside of class communication. Would 1000% recommend for all students to take him at least once!

The underclassmen reviewing Larry don't appreciate his policies and whine until they hit the upper levels. His descriptive project descriptions are a literal GODSEND, just be competent and read through the pages to know exactly how to get a 100. He also posts all the resources that you need to easily get an A in this class. People act like you're forced to do all 30 pages of his practice exams, but they should be grateful that there's so many resources and don't have to suffer through a class without a single released practice exam. His lectures can be a little boring, but he releases great slides afterwards. Take this class if you want the best optimization between grades and material learned.

Hardest part of this class is not falling asleep during lecture. Goes pretty in depth and teaches to an pretty well. Just make sure to attend class and take notes. Projects are annoying but aren't difficult.

I like Larry, he prepares you really well for the exams because he gives very accurate practice exams. He is pretty good at lecturing (concepts are explained very well and thoroughly) but you need to make sure to pay attention because his slides aren't that helpful. Not gonna lie I fell asleep in class almost every lecture due to his monotonous voice (and saw many of my classmates doing the same), and due to that I had to spend extra time studying. I got an A regardless, but if you actually stay awake you should be pretty well-prepared. The project descriptions are very thorough, and if you read them fully you should get a 100% on every one. The extra credit on projects and class participation points are good grade boosters, but majority of your grade relies on your exam scores. Definitely take Larry if you have to pick between him and Burkhauser -- we had much higher exam averages and I've heard Burkhauser's teaching style is not the best.

Larry is a good professor if YOU DO THE WORK. So his class isn't easy. His lectures aren't mandatory but some times he will pass out worksheets in lectures and will give posts based on completion(even if u didn't complete it u can get 100). I missed one worksheet and it dropped my grade by .8% which is a lot at the end of the semester. Second PLEASE GO TO DISCUSSIONS. Also his projects aren't hard just start early. They also have extra credit if u submit once and 2 days before the submit date. I suggest you go for these since it helps in the long run. Also you have to participate twice that's it. Third, the tests are like the practice tests. Please study the practice tests its so helpful and he does a amazing job making sure the practice test lines up with the real test. About larry as an instructor. His classes are fine but boring. He teaches the content well and explains it well but CMSC132 is just boring.

Unsung hero of UMD. Anyone complaining about him is in for a rough ride for the rest of their major. His grading policies are a little nitpicky but valid. His lectures are very in-depth and will cover everything on the exam. Free points, and he genuinely cares about his students. He doesn't let students use tech in class, so just sit in the back if you use an iPad to take notes. Take Herman if you want to learn and can appreciate awkward and lowk autistic humor.

I felt that Mr. Herman was an amazing instructor. I know his sense of humor isn't for everyone as it can be a bit dry, but you can tell he cares about the class and wants everyone to succeed. To succeed in the course and get a great grade I will give this advice: 1. Always show up to lecture. He doesn't post videos about his lectures and it is so valuable anyways to see him talk through the problems in front of the class. 2. Get on projects early. He gives this advice throughout the semester but I can't stress it enough, one of the keys to my success in this course was doing the projects early, picking up the 6 points of extra credit, and by taking my time and writing good student tests, I consistently got 106's on the projects, which gives you a lot of wiggle room to mess up on exams. 3. Do the practice exams. Going along with point 2, do those projects, you also need to do the practice exams. His review is incredibly helpful and while it may be intimidating to tackle a 20 something pages review document, by the end of that you are going to be a pro and hopefully ace the exam. He will talk a lot in class about the importance of doing the review and he is not kidding. In my opinion it is most helpful for the MCQ section because those can have some tricky questions, but the practice exam almost certainly contains similar questions, or at least guides you on what you need to learn. If you do these three things I feel you will be very prepared and his class should be a pretty straight-forward A. So concluding about Mr. Herman: - He posts super helpful review material and valuable projects - His lectures will help you learn the material really well - He loves frogs (a lot!)

Nora is a nice person and will try to be as flexible as possible when it comes to assignments and quizzes and exams and such for dates and grading, as we had a curve at the end for our final exam and the final grade, for example. However, given that it is her first time teaching at the college level, she is not a good lecturer at all. She did not seem familiar with the content and when she would try to explain things it just seemed vague and like a whole lot of jargon that did not really make any sense. It also seemed like her lectures were directly derived from the slides and any deviation from them, such as if someone asked a question, would result in her likely saying "I don't know" or "I'm not sure" or "Let's find out", which is often times not very helpful. The discussions are alright but do not always go over the concepts taught in class in an overlapping way. The content covered in the class is not too difficult in my opinion as there are so many good resources better than lecture itself that go over things like data structures and other concepts discussed which was a great help. With some time, I think she could be a great professor, however, she is not a good professor as of now.

I would like to start by saying that I went to almost all of her lectures, and I saw a LOT of new faces at the final exam. She never intended to record the lectures, she didn't have to, she did it because WE asked her, she did her best to accommodate us. So if these students didn't feel like showing up to her lectures, that is on them not on her. I think she didn't record 2-3 lectures. When I took Pedram, he didn't record 2 lectures, so there is that. I am sensing a lot of these reviews are from students who didn't go to her lectures because there is a lot of misinformation in these reviews. She apologized for the second midterm, and besides that one, all of her exams were easy and exactly like the practice exams. I took her because I heard bad things about the other teacher and I made a risky decision to take a new professor instead of the one that has been teaching for years. I see a lot of A's for a teacher that has such bad exams. If she was as bad as all these reviews make her seem to be, then they had 2 weeks to switch to the other teacher (he had A LOT of open seats and does not record lectures), just saying. They were hoping for a NEW teacher who would have 10 years of experience of being a lecturer. Instead they got a NEW teacher that made some mistakes. Shocker. I am giving her 4/5 stars because she could have explained things a little better and because of the projects being delayed so much. To anyone trying to decide whether to take her or not, all of her mistakes were caused because it was her first semester, so they will probably not happen again. I think that she will be fine starting the fall of 2025, when she will have 2 semesters experience.

Larry is a great professor. It is true that he speaks slow sometimes and I can understand if people find it boring, but overall I think he is great at explaining subjects. If you go to lecture you can trust that he will teach you everything you need to know. MIdterms and Final were fair + he gave a lot of good practice material beforehand.

Incredibly easy class but a really bad professor. If you red to be spoon fed information do not take this class you will fail. If you can learn yourself, also figure out what how she teaches it because they are relatively strict with the grading and want code written a certain way.

I feel absolutely awful writing this review. Nora is such a sweetheart and really does her best to help. She just... really doesn't know what she's talking about. I can't count the number of times she's messed up in lecture or seemed more confused than any of her students. This was largely a self taught course, considering she would rush through slides with minimal explanation, just odd tangents loosely related to the material. The course was also improperly paced with our class never doing an official project on Threads or Graphs, two essential concepts for later CS courses. The exams were far from impossible in their actual content but we were so ill-prepared for them that the averages consistently hung in the high 70s range. Nora, if you're reading this, I'm sorry. You're truly such a kind human being. But please, brush up on your concepts, maybe make some speaker notes for lecture and write your exams far ahead of time so you know what to prepare students for. I'd also like to mention that she dealt with a family emergency during the semester, moving houses, and a large transition from high school to college so she really wasn't at her best. Hopefully future semesters will be better but for now, I'd select the other option if given a choice.

I think as a professor, Herman was boring BUT really nice. If you go to class and pay attention because there are no recordings! Also, go to the lab because a lot of the stuff in the lab was on the exams. The multiple-choice exams were weird points-wise. Everything is logical, if you understand the concepts you're in good shape to do the projects and the exams, if not go to TA office hours PLEASE, you can learn it by yourself too but I would advise you to go to office hours. Try to do well on the midterms(they shouldn't be that bad if you do the projects and the lab sheets) because the final was pretty hard.

Didn'y rlly like his sense of humor or attitude towards students. Would constantly repeat himself when discussing class policies or telling students what not to do. Also seems to take joy from calling students out for using phones during class and making them embarrassed. He also password protects any and all docs on elms which was annoying but minor. His lectures are boring but attendance is mandatory (via graded worksheets on random days) and occasionally he emphasizes a point that will be on the test. His lecture examples were good and honestly you will have a rlly good grasp on the content if you take him. Lots of projects throughout the semester which I enjoyed and there was also a couple extra credit options for turning in early. His exams were ok the only true hard part was his multiple choice where he doesn't tell you how many options are right. He does provide a lot of practice material and optional hws which you can use to study.

Overall a good lecturer. Has some weird quirks and at times can appear rude. Pros - Well written project descriptions. He is very clear about the requirements. Only negative about this is that the project descriptions are long, and you need to read them carefully multiple times. - Explains well during class if you pay attention. Repeats important concepts multiple times. - Gives a lot of practice material through discussion work sheets, homework, and practice exams. Projects also force you to practice actually coding what he's teaching in class. - Exams are very fair and fairly easy. Only exception is the final which had harder questions than any of the 3 midterms. Neutral - Has a lot of weird policies and procedures you must follow. For examples no phones in class and no sitting in the back two rows; he will stop lecture every time and call you out if he sees this happening. Although all of his policies make sense given the circumstances in which he's teaching, it is a lot to keep track of. - You can not use ELMS or email to message him. He says this is because he has a lot of students, which he does, and to contact him instead during office hours. He does have office hours 3 times a week, all of which are available via zoom, but it is kind of inconvenient to only be able to contact him at such specific times. Cons - Does not have official answer key for discussion worksheets, so you kinda just have to trust the TA, who isn't as experienced as him. - Can be rude if you don't follow his exact policies and procedures.

Teaches well and is very clear on what is expected in the exams, projects, and worksheets. Policies are really annoying, have to attend every lecture cause there could be a graded WS (but its free points if you show up), for me the exams were difficult, doesn't grade work in time, and very strict about style in project. Overall, not a bad class, learned a lot and nice to have an organized professor that makes expectations clear. Definitely not an easy A.

Pros: - The quizzes (as the semester went on) were online and had unlimited attempts - She usually lets class out around 20 minutes early - Records lectures (when she remembers) and posts the slides Cons: - The projects took a long time, and often had nothing to do with the content we were actually learning. For example, there was a project on HTML (which had nothing to do with our quizzes or exams) that took an extremely long time and was due the night before an exam. Pretty much the only project that did relate to the content we were tested on was binary search trees, and it was given after we already had an exam on it - The exams, especially the 2nd one, were nothing like the practice exams that were given - The lecturing was very unorganized and she would often get confused with the code and ask students what they thought was wrong with it, especially in the first month or so of the class Overall, the class on a day-to-day basis felt easier than 131 but when it came time for exams, it was incredibly hard to prepare yourself. You basically had to teach yourself all of the content because you aren't learning it in lecture, and the projects aren't organized in a way where you can learn it from them either. The slides that she posts were pretty in-depth and easy to follow which helped, but they alone don't prepare you adequately for the exams (need to watch youtube videos and find practice problems, etc.). She did seem like a nice and chill person during lectures, but would then threaten to give you a 0 if you wrote one extra word after the exam time had ended (when she explicitly stated that she made the exam too long lol). Hopefully she gets better considering this is only her first semester teaching at UMD, but as of right now I would try to take someone else if you can.

Alright Alright Alright- The way she articulates her words makes the content impossible to understand. I had Pedram last semester and was able to get an A using just the lectures he gave, but you cannot succeed in this class without hours of self-teaching. Also, no, the projects do not take "a couple hours" They take days. Most students do not know HTML, so that took forever. Sadly, many cs kids are insecure about their intelligence and believe glazing burk(giving 5 star reviews) and saying the class is easy makes them look smarter. Also, there is a TA named david and he is evil. Someone will ask a normal question on piazza and he either gives a completely condescending response or just tells the student he is not "required" to help. Mans needs a gf.

Chill person to talk to, but I completely would recommend you have your bases covered (everything from cmsc131 ), this class shifts from a programming class to a dsa class around the 4th week, which is a mental shock to many. This shock lasts for a few weeks, so I wouldn't depend on the prof as a crutch if you don't understand things, try and use online resources and the slides to study consistently. That will reduce the stress of compounded exam + project weeks. It will also help for finals. I don't think the class is incredibly difficult, but it is somewhat hard. Exams averages are around C's, so as long as you attend discussion, do the worksheets there (Christina is my goat), and review consistently, you should jump the hurdle that is this weeder course!
Learn it all urself, not a hard class but it sucks if you just have to do it all urself

While these reviews are unfair, they are not unfounded. While her lectures are okay, they really don't teach much. Yes, this is her first time teaching as a college professor, and yes, it is her first time using Java. The quizzes vary wildly in difficulty, and the class felt fairly disorganized. Projects for her course were okay though, and typically never took too long. I highly recommend another professor, but if you can't take any other, Nora is acceptable. If you spend the time to learn on your own, you should do well in this course.

Honestly, I've heard a lot of terrible things about Larry before taking this class, so I was expecting the worst. He is weirdly strict about certain things, like sitting in the back of the lecture hall (which I found especially annoying if I ever came in late for whatever reason), or the random worksheets. The worksheets itself are an easy 10 points! It was just a bit frustrating because it added more pressure and anxiety if you ever needed to miss a lecture. I also found the participation points annoying, especially for a course of this size. As for his lectures, I found them incredibly helpful. The material could be a bit heavy at times, but I really do feel like I learned a lot from this class. The way he sets up this course is truly set up for YOUR success and learning. As long as you actually pay attention to his lectures, take notes and ask questions when you are confused, you will do well in this class. Projects were pretty straight forward. The PDFs were dense, but it was clear what was expected and necessary to pass secret tests. The main reasons why I am not getting a better grade in this class is not asking questions or for clarification when I needed it the most. I cannot stress enough: if you are confused, go to office hours and ask!! You can absolutely succeed in this course if you do that. Overall, a good instructor, just a bit tedious at times. TLDR; Good at teaching, has a few annoying rules, but it is very possible to well in this class.

Class was very manageable and the tests were not very difficult. Despite what reviews say, if you know your stuff you should be good. The practice tests she gives where actually a great guide for the real exams, and I thought the real exams were fairly straightforward. The quizzes initially are worded pretty bad, but she fixed it later in the semester.

She does not know the content she is teaching. You are better off not going to lectures and reading through the slides. She makes simple, understandable topics confusing and convoluted. She often gives incorrect information while teaching and cannot answer even the most basic questions on the topics she is teaching. She takes almost a full lecture to explain a topic that a 10-minute YouTube video would explain better. She is a literal high school teacher and should not be teaching this class. You are better off taking another professor.
Mid grade for a mid professor. Very infuriating policy (no electronics, password-protected documents), and difficult exams. Boring lecturer. Only redeeming quality is well-written and clear projects.
lost points because i had a space that went past the 80 character limit. how am i supposed to see that

He makes all of his documents password protected which means every time you open one of his pdfs you need to enter his stupid password over and over again and by the end of the sem its the most infuriating thing to type it in. Decent professor but you'll be subject to more of these weird rules and restrictions across the course.

I don't know how there are 5-star reviews because the experience has NOT been the same. The average on the exams are around Cs and C+s, which are not like the samples she gives. I think she is lenient with dropping the grades, but I still do not recommend taking the class with her. She does not record the lectures properly. She didn't have recordings for important topics which threw everyone off. Please please please do not take her if you have the choice to.
