Each topic is explained in great detail, there is a ton of resources provided outside of lecture to study and review. The course itself is also quite fun, but can be challenging. Also open note midterms and final is pretty great. Highly recommend taking him whenever possible.

He teaches pretty well, you come out of the class with a good understanding of 216 content. The worst part about this class are the projects, they're long and hard, start early. Tons of people get caught using GPT for the projects, but he's talked about somehow implementing it for future semesters to be more AI friendly/accommodating. Final Grade average: 85.58 Final Grade median: 87.79

All of the projects, homeworks, labs are very useful and relevant for the exams. Nothing in this class is that difficult, but make sure to start on all projects as soon as possible. Kauffman is always heavy on catching cheating on the projects; just give them your best honest attempt and I promise you'll be fine. His slides are very wordy and often contain a lot of useless information, and you're meant to rely more on your own work for the exams (they are open resource). Kauffman is by far the best choice for 216 right now and it's not really close.

A phenomenal amazing passionate considerate professor. Kauffman provides you with all the resources you need to succeed. He clearly cares a lot. - incredibly fair exams (open note too) - detailed and well-structured projects - very quick Piazza responses - lecture and lab/project walkthrough videos on Panopto - leniency with some late projects - so many opportunities for bonus points / extra credit / ways to save your grade I’d recommend him 1000x

Amazing Professor! Lectures are very engaging and he explains things clearly. Grading is very fair and he gives lots of opportunities for bonus/make up credit. Labs and HWs are relatively easy and focus on introducing concepts. Projects are hard but not impossible and they truly teach you the material. Exams are fairly difficult but they do reflect what's taught in class and are similar to the practice exams given. If you study hard and understand the labs and projects, you can definitely do well on the exams. Re doing labs on paper or parts of projects is a great way to study, the exams usually have pretty normal coding questions with the rest being short answer. The short answer matters just as much as the coding in terms of grading which isn't bad by any means. He gives extra credit for participating in lectures and answering questions on piazza which is a great idea!! I initially was motivated to participate for the extra credit but after the first few weeks of class, I found myself participating out of genuine curiosity or just wanting to engage with the material. For piazza, this was the one class where students would always respond to questions quickly which was very helpful. Overall, Kauffman is a great professor and I'm glad I got to take this class with him!

There's a lot of things that Kauffman does well like the format of the exams (open note, open terminal) and the instructiveness of the projects (the project descriptions are very thorough). He also records lectures and really tries to make sure that you can do well in the class. However, the main reason I took off 3 stars is because of all the AI accusations he was throwing around. Like I was not accused, but I know so many people who were falsely accused of using AI and that literally made it so scary just to go to class. Even though I wasn't doing anything wrong, I always felt like I would be falsely accused. I think that this part of the course needs to change because while it is important to ensure that the class is fair for students who actually do their work, I think that before Kauffman goes around accusing people, he needs to be 100% sure they actually did something wrong.

Kauffman is an incredible professor and 216 is pretty easy with him. His lectures are incredibly detailed and informative. The structure of the class is very well made and makes it easy to understand everything. Every assignment given gives enough practice for the exams (you should not chatgpt the projects for this reason) so you don't need to practice much outside of the coursework and if you didn't understand something in lecture the labs and projects will usually fill these gaps. The projects only have public tests and the exams are open laptop so you can download anything you want for the exam just no google or ai. One warning I'll give is he told us he would reorganize the grading structure to deal with ai cheating and his options were either lower project weighting and raise exam weighting, add an extra midterm, or automatically fail anyone who's exam average is below 70%. Personally I don't think these should be an issue due to what I've written in this review. If you're reading this and picking classes definitely take this guy over anyone else

Genuinely horific class. There's a ton of reviews that elaborate on why he's so terrible but it really does not compare to how awful it is to actually be in this class. Please, PLEASE do not take Yoon if you can help it. Hell, even if you would have to delay taking this class, that might be preferable if you're already struggling mentally. I mean this seriously. Projects are poorly documented and extremely tedious. Classes are unbelievably boring and nigh unfollowable with how poorly Yoon can actually qualify what he is teaching. Quizzes and exams consist of either extremely tedious coding, very niche knowledge, and the memorization that'll have you wondering whether anything you learn in this class will actually stick with you: it won't. On multiple ocassions you will think this class has drained your passion for CS. I can know state with certain that I still like CS, but that Yoon has doon irreperable damage to my view of UMD and any form of education it could teach me in the subjects that actually matter to me. If this isn't enough to convince you, Kaufman apparently has open notes. Avoid Yoon at all cost.

Nicole is the only reason why I am still alive in 216 w/ Yoon. She is super nice and helpful and I wish she was our lecturer because her explanations always make 100x more sense than those given by Yoon. She is goated

I genuinely like Yoon as a professor and of the CS professors I've had at UMD so far, Yoon is my favorite. Though it's obvious I'm odd in that. He's really nice and will happily answer any questions you have, and its very clear he cares about teaching the class and students success. I don't think there's anything that feels unfair at any point, and he often puts up clarifications (or honestly borderline hints) for exams/quizzes part way through for common clarifications. I think the projects could have clearer explanations on the assignment docs, but I think some of that comes down to real-world edge cases or behavior vs what they want to and can test in a class project sometimes not lining up exactly. I will say, the TAs were very hit or miss this semester and any real complaints I do have about the course come down to that, since the grading can take a while and some of the responses on piazza were not the best.

He's quirky but a great lecturer (not a professor). Our exam Averages were lower than Kaufman who was teaching 216 at the same time, which is something to keep in mind. But imo the exams were generally difficult, but fair and he gives you a lot of resources to prepare for them ahead of time.
Larry Herman kindled my love for systems programming. He models what a lecturer should be: engaging and clear. I see now after many years that he's one of UMD's best.
First exam took me from feeling like I'd get an A to feeling like "lets pray for a curve to a C-" even after doing great on the quizzes. Class is abuse.

I want to say: I've gotten 90-100% on everything so far, this isn't a grade thing. The only avg I can see for quizzes so far is quiz1, which is ~20/30 so right in line with what people say of "most people technically fail every assessment.", so take my own grade with a grain of short term salt. I hate writing reviews mid-semester for classes since I think it can come off as salty, but given he's teaching the class again in Spring I must do my part for students registering for classes lol. Yoon is actually a nice person. BUT. The class quality is genuinely atrocious, I wanted to disagree with the previous reviews but really the fact that this class is costing me money is rather depressing. I don't personally have any issue with the lectures but I also don't pay any attention during them, since skimming the slides is usually plenty, but that really doesn't excuse the teaching quality. The projects aren't that bad, as long as you sit down and do them with a couple days of time to work since they can involve a lot of writing code even if its straightforward. My only issue with the projects is the same issue I have with quizzes and assume I will have with the exams: the requirements (or on assessments the phrasing of questions) is usually so poor that they simply don't make sense or are missing key details for you to do the work. It's been the case on every project so far that there is an absolute deluge of posts asking "what does this actually mean" about project specs, because the phrasing of a spec will be vague enough that you could interpret it many different ways and the public tests will be so incredibly simple that they don't address a single one of the clarifications needed. There's also often not actually any details given on expected formatting for things that require formatting, and TAs/instructor will sometimes give conflicting answers with each other to questions. Whether I end this class with a C- or an A+ I'll leave having learned the exact same amount from the lectures and projects: absolutely nothing. The fact UMD is ranked as high as it is for CS and then has professors for lower level classes that cannot teach and TAs which vary from "make the class easy as pie" to "Lecture round 2 with no benefit to the students at all" is rather insane. You would think it in the school's interest to attempt to give students a strong foundation so they can confidently get internships, do research, do well in later classes, but I'm pretty sure the avg student coming out of this class will only have gained the knowledge that "they don't like C/assembly" based on a bad prof.

Oh Yoon, Oh Yoon, where do I start? This was single-handedly the most BEAUTIFUL class I've ever taken during my long, long, 7 years at College Park, Maryland. Yoon explains things so beautifully even a monkey could understand assembly and linux. I'm convinced the 85% of the class that gets below an A has an iq between 41 and 67. I showed my pet goldfish the Yoon lectures (he didn't record them, so I uploaded them myself using my neuralink) and he showed up to the lecture and got a 97 on the final. He invoked such an incredible passion for low-level programming in me I decided to skip the entirety of my undergrad and do a PHD at Tsinghua in compilers. I came to class every day, and he handed me extra points for simply existing, because he's such a beautiful soul. GOON WITH YOON! #YOONERS
Super nice professor but I'm not sure if I really learned anything because his exams were practice test simulators. Projects aren't too bad tho! He's also very entertaining to watch.

honestly this class was pretty hard in the sense that i swear not being able to use computers or phones in class made it impossible to follow along with the code he taught us. on top of that lectures were so hard to follow because it was so slow and monotone other than the slight jokes he made. his worksheets were completion which was helpful but they were random so you had to attend every lecture and discussion or else youd get a zero not to mention you literally can not email him at all or he gets so upset, everything gotta be at his office hours or for absenses you have to fill out a form so its kind of difficult to communicate but he was funny and to be fair his tests werent too hard to pass, and his project guidelines were extremely thourough (for them being 10 pages long minimum id expect them to be) as long as you go to every single lecture since he doesnt post his stuff other than very vague slideshows anywhere

taking the summer session currently, and it's making me want to kill myself! lectures are ramble-y, examples are far too busy, and the way we're being taught assembly (ie reading off slides verbatim) is absurd. exams and earlier projects were light but holy shit is this class miserable currently

Really well ran course. The projects were pretty instructive, especially the Assembly project. Considering that the tests are borderline open internet, they're surprisingly easy, and if you can do the projects, you can definitely do well on the exams. There's so many places to get bonus points, so even if you lose some points here or there, you can get them back really easily. The lectures can be a bit slow at times, but physical attendance isn't mandatory so if that's an issue you can just watch the lecture videos at your own pace. He loves answering questions, so if you're struggling with a concept, he'll make sure you understand it. Taking 216 with Kauffman really is a no-brainer, just do it.

If you want an easy grade without really learning anything, take Nelson. He tries to be funny, but that's not what I came to school for. I wish I had taken 216 with somebody who actually cares more about teaching than just being popular. I got an A, but I don't really feel prepared for upper level courses that might depend on 216.

pros: smart dude, good demonstrations, worksheets,projects,participation is grade padding, and final exam questions are very similar to the midterm and practice exams. cons: no phones no laptops during lecture, he wastes probably 5 minutes each class stuttering or calling people out for using their phone. unforgiving n strict, slow at grading, he gatekeeps online communication and office hours behind a fat block of text and rules u gotta read (this one not that bad just annoying). multiple choice section on exams is notoriously difficult and specific, he also DOCKS POINTS FOR WRONG ANSWERS which is apparently to "discourage guessing" but inherently makes every second guess themself lol. we had a failing midterm average. lectures are also incredibly boring but thats expected for any cs class ig. he did curve final grades idk what the dude 2 below is sayin looking back i would take an 8am to avoid larry in a heartbeat but thats jus me

One of the best professors I have ever had. I think he truly structures his course through a student's perspective. The fact that he records his lectures and lab walkthroughs really allowed me to fully understand the course concepts. I feel like he gave adequate practice materials for exams and although they were REALLY long, his projects were one of the biggest reasons I did well in this course. His long and intensive projects covered basically everything that we would need to know for the course and doing the projects helped me not only understand the topics conceptually but also in practice. My advice to future students would be to really understand the projects because thats usually what the coding question for exams will be based off of.

One of the best professors I've had yet. I thought his lectures were well-paced, informative and easy to understand. He is an engaging and personable lecturer who takes time to answer questions and his participation system provides a nice padding to your overall grade if you happen to need that. The later projects were ROUGH but not impossibly difficult with a reasonable amount of effort, starting on them early is definitely for the best. His open note exams (which I greatly preferred) test your ability to apply concepts instead of memorize random details, for obvious reasons. I def think your grade reflects the amount of effort you put in, and honestly this class was much less stressful than 132 for me.

Great professor who cares about his students' learning. Great class structure with lots of flexibility, difficult but rewarding projects, and easy but not free exams. You get what you put into this class.

Even though I struggled tremendously in this course, Kauffman seems to care about his students' success. He is especially engaging in lectures and extremely helpful during office hours and the staff are always there to help on OH, Piazza, and Discussion. I believe that if you put in the effort and dedication in this course, you will be able to do well. I will say, though, that you should start ALL of Kauffman's projects on day 1 because these projects are not easy, and they require a lot of focus and attention, and you will have to know the content very well. In addition, his exams are not easy, so make sure you really understand your notes, slides, projects, lecture code, etc. I will give him 3 stars, however, because he does make this class somewhat stressful, but I do think that these stressful experiences will continue in later CMSC classes (not to say that they're bad, but they will become more rigorous, and students will need to work harder to do well). Kauffman provides tremendous grade padding from manual inspection on projects and a fair engagement point system, which can tremendously help students improve their grades. If you are someone who is interested in low-level systems, then I believe that you should definitely take 216 with Kauffman, and you will like it !

He gives a lot of examples, but the multiple choice on his exams kill me lol. I think his one star ratings are wayyy too harsh. I didn’t study as much as I should have and that’s why I got a bad grade. GO TO DISCUSSION. Omg please the worksheets are helpful! Also, he goes into DETAIL for his project requirements. Read everything. My biggest issue with him was that I find his voice very easy to sleep to in lecture lol.
brother didnt curve this terrible class lmao last straw

as someone with 9 years of programming experience here are the pros and cons of larry the lecturer not professor: tldr: will you fail? only if you dont try your best pros: - practice exams cover most relevant material (enough to get an A) - he will actually give you all the material/instructions - he will repeat material that will be on a test multiple times in a row during lecture - all of the material is important (some people were falling asleep, this is the basics of computers, get used to it) - wont argue about ADS accommodations (just visit his office hours) cons: - annoying policies (not talking about attending class or electronics, that is normal. youre paying for the lecture hall, go to it??) - teaches some content only from you reading the textbook (only in 216) - slow at grading, not the TAs fault, hes just slow - wont let you email him (at least hes nice in office hours) - rambles a bit

Goes on rants during class and wastes valuable class time correcting students on their phones and trying to pick up from where he left off. He wastes like an average of 2 minutes per lecture doing this. Projects are horrendously long, he likes to throw stupid curveball questions on exams, won't teach certain chapters from the book and just assigns it as reading and then tests hard on it on exams like you're a jackass with a blindfold. Discussions are pointless besides picking up the worksheet, which most of the time can be done alone outside of discussion time. If you have a tight, hard schedule in your semester coming up, don't take this guy as he's like a greedy goblin taking up your valuable study time like it's gold.

Actually the worst professor ever. Teaches useless shit during lecture and repeats the same useless information over and over again. Forced us to learn assembly and other important concepts on our own. Exams are nothing like the practice. Averages on both midterms were 65 but blames the class instead when it says more about him. It’s been a week since the final and I still don’t know if I’ll pass the class or if he will curve like he said he would. What professor forces students to learn the main topics of the class on their own?

One of the worst experiences of my life. It's been a week since the final exam and I AM STILL WAITING to know if I pass this class or not. Whatever you do, AVOID HERMAN at all costs. Don't listen to the 5 star reviews gassing im up as they don't know what they're talking about. Herman made my second semester at UMD a living hell. Please do yourself a favor and avoid this prof.

Kauffman is so goated and beloved by all his students. He made content that I otherwise would've found relatively uninteresting (operating systems, unix processes, assembly) engaging. He is incredibly passionate about teaching and always takes time to answer questions during lecture, and also post lecture recordings as well as lab overviews. I learned so much from him this semester and you can tell he actually cares about making sure his students understand content. The projects can be difficult, and I'd recommend starting them early, as well as using Piazza as much as you can because office hours are pretty limited. Exams are also tough but his homeworks and labs give you good practice for them. Most people probably don't actually do the homework but if you want extra practice then you should do it. He also gives opportunities for extra credit as mentioned in other reviews. I think there is no better professor you can take for CMSC216 and with Kauffman you'll be in good hands.

I took Kauffman in Fall 2024, but I'm just writing my review now. He's a nice guy and is very knowledgeable about what he's teaching, but I'm gonna be honest people are glazing him way too much. First of all, he speaks very technically in class with heavy technical jargon and doesn’t break down new concepts in a digestible way, which makes it hard to follow. Additionally, his slides have so much content on them that it's hard to digest and understand what exactly you're supposed to look at. The good thing though, is that he gives lots of extra credit, which is pretty rare to see in a CS class, so that’s nice. Another thing is, no offense to him, but he seems a little socially awkward. I talked to him in office hours and after class, and he seems to have a hard time speaking at a normal speed and has trouble making eye contact (another thing I’ve noticed with a lot of CS professors at UMD—a lot of them seem autistic or socially awkward). Another thing is that he has a lot of trouble taking criticism. In his midterm survey, so many students pointed out that there is a big difference in the level of difficulty between his practice and real exams, but he claims that because he has way more experience and is smarter than us, he knows a lot more than we do and dismisses literally any criticism. Overall, you will get a good grade in this class if you put in the effort, but him as a professor is not the best choice.

It's clear that the following three reviews were all written by the same disgruntled student. Please take these with a grain of salt and do your own research before making any decisions. Personally, I had no issues with Kauffman and found him to be one of the best teachers in the CS department at this school. >> Honestly I haven't learned anything in his class for cmsc216 because he would never explain things properly due to his unintelligible English; his notes, slides, labs, and projects didn't help and he would purposefully put tricky questions on exams just to assume that people are cheating in his class. DO NOT take this class as it will ruin your emotional stability and cause immense paranoia like it did to me. Stay away from this man and protect yourself at ALL COSTS !!!!! >> very difficult and unhelpful lectures >> I don't think I understood much from Kauffman's lectures. His slides and lecture code was often hard to follow and he information overloads on his project docs, slides, and lab/hmw directions. Although some may find it helpful with given information, I often had a hard time understanding what exactly I am supposed to do with the given information and how I can learn/complete the tasks. I found his projects to be mentally breaking, and I don't agree that this is what is best for students. I believe this class took a huge toll on my mental health and emotional stability. but I don't think Kauffman's 216 would be a great learning experience as you would have to struggle for basically nothing. Last but not least, I don't even understand why we had to learn Assembly in his class because I find Assembly to be completely useless and no one uses it anymore in modern programming. Take this class with another professor if you can

Honestly I haven't learned anything in his class for cmsc216 because he would never explain things properly due to his unintelligible English; his notes, slides, labs, and projects didn't help and he would purposefully put tricky questions on exams just to assume that people are cheating in his class. DO NOT take this class as it will ruin your emotional stability and cause immense paranoia like it did to me. Stay away from this man and protect yourself at ALL COSTS !!!!!

If I could rate the guy higher than 5 I would. Prof K has probably the most amount of passion for teaching students I’ve witnessed. Expect a lot of work with weekly labs and homework’s in addition to 5 projects, but all of which help a ton with learning the content. Prof K rarely was unable to fully answer a question during lecture and he absolutely knows his stuff. Definitely recommend if you can take him.

Idk why his rating is so low tbh. Larry is super fair with the stuff he teaches if you go to lecture and actually pay attention you will be perfectly prepared for the exams. His projects are great because the descriptions get real specific about what you should do while also leaving some room for you to do it your way. 10 total points were free points of worksheets graded on completion that you just had to go to some lectures/discussions to complete. On top of this he curves. Getting an A in this class is easy if you regularly attend lecture and are half decent at programming.

Kauffman and I had our enemies to lovers arc this semester. At the beginning of the semester with the stuff introducing C, I felt he went too slow, I felt the labs were unhelpful, and I felt bored in the class. But once Kauffman got to Assembly, and especially once he got to the UNIX and hardware material at the end of the semester, he really opened my eyes to learning about computer systems in general. Professor Kauffman has an enthusiastic lecturing style---you can tell he cares a lot about our understanding of the material, and the labs and homeworks he provides really do give great practice for his exams. His exams are also challenging, but definitely fair, and the open resources were a nice touch that I genuinely used, albeit barely, but when I used it it was absolutely needed. There was never a question I did not know how to do on his exams given the practice material provides you with exact question types you might see. Professor Kauffman has a plethora of pop culture references as well which keep him feeling simultaneously like your old uncle and someone in the know. I would recommend anyone take Professor Kauffman if you value your learning and your education more than your grade. His projects are difficult, but manageable if you start early, and I never had problems submitting on time nor with the actual coding itself. If you put in the work, you will get the A you desire. I have to dock one star because he called my supreme overlord Larry Herman "Professor" Herman. Everyone knows the GOAT is an instructor, NOT a professor.

Seeing a lot of people downvote Larry just because he's one of the first CS professors that they have at UMD. Larry will literally baby and hold your hand throughout this whole course- he does this by giving discussion worksheets where problems are literally pulled for the exam, LONG practice material documents with questions the exam same format as the exam, and LONG LONG LONG project descriptions that highlight practically every single edge case that you ever need to consider. The worksheets are literally just free padding to your grade (just show up!), so I'm not sure why people complain about that. They're literally not even graded for correctness, it's just like do 3/4 of the worksheet and boom instant 10/10. Larry tells you every single thing that you need to know and even how to know it (like just reading chapter X in the specified textbook), and the people complaining here are just the ones too lazy to do it.

Do not take his class. Extremely boring lecturer who goes incredibly in depth on the topics and then expects you to pull out the most random piece of knowledge for his exams. His exams are also incredibly difficult, and he will punish you for not knowing the material by taking off additional points if you get some questions wrong. He then has the audacity to be surprised at why the class average is so low. You also can't skip class because there is a slight possibility of a worksheet in class, which is ultimately useless if you haven't been keeping up in the first place. The most I ever learned from this class was by doing the projects (they did teach me a lot about programming in C). Still, he also makes them incredibly over the top, and his directions are unclear, including unnecessary sentences, detracting from the main requirements or criteria you need to follow. That being said, his projects were generally easy to complete or learn to complete.

Herman is a great 132 teacher, but for the love of god, he CANNOT teach 216. 216 is already a hard course, but herman will make it harder. He will make it a living hell. Unannounced worksheets in every other lecture/discussion that if you miss enough can bring your grade down more than a project. Exams are nothing like practice material. Save yourself the trouble and avoid herman at all cost

I would absolutely take Kauffman again--you can absolutely tell he loves teaching and his students, and he comes off as very approachable (which is really nice when you have a question, over email or in person). The class / projects / tests are hard, but it's absolutely worth the time and effort. Has a really good engagement point system that gives you some grace on late project submissions if you, again, put in the effort. Very well organized system, posts all his slides, records all his lectures, etc. etc.

Decent lecturer. Can be condescending and say things are "easy" or "simple" about concepts students see for the first time which is not helpful to students learning. Released project 4 over a week late with a short deadline and project had so much testing bugs and issues that Piazza had hundreds of posts. Not sure why there are such high reviews.

no way people are giving this dude above 3 stars- the most boring lectures the weirdest exams with a mean and median of 60 please don't take him save yourself he doesn't teach jack expects you to read entire chapters before coming to lecture its a self learn class with ass exams

i had yoon for both 132 and 216, i feel like people who usually give reviews are losers but i have a hot take about yoon, honestly class isn't that hard, ik a lot of people do bad, but if you think you're semi smart you should be fine, both 132 and 216 i would learn the whole material like day before test and do fine, projects are usually not bad, every now and then there's a hard one, but its usually easy, 132 was genuinely not that bad, yoon is known for asking a lot of specific details, but for 132 as long as u get decent indepth understanding of the data structures and assumptions behind them, its not that bad, 216 midterms were honestly kinda hard cus c is known for having a lot of weird nuances abt it, but usually the coding problems were p easy, my hot take is yoon is a terrible teacher but people somewhat like him cus he seems like a nice guy but he isn't actually that nice, he's pretty rigid with the rules, in my experience he doesn't give late extensions unless you have a doctors note before, i feel like most people wouldnt think its that bad but i think its kinda crazy imagine ur dying sick like ur teacher really gonna make you get out of bed and get a doctors note from across the campus and a sometimes you can't even get doctors note one 1 day notice, honestly i didnt care abt this until i realized all the other teachers usually give u the benefit of the doubt, atp i feel like yoon doesnt have too much going for him, hes probably a nice guy tbh, but he doesn't really give u any slack, overall id say very rigid with rules, doesn't teach very well, not that hard curriculum, good lecture slides and easy to self study usually, and not too hard to get like a b range grade, kinda hard to get an a though midterms were kinda easy, final for 216 was hard assembly projects were easy, i lowk had a very surface level understanding of assembly so i didn't do great in final, but overall id say very doable class, also ik a lot of people say its the most difficult class, idk if id disagree with that statement, but i really dont think it was that bad, like i wouldnt say i put in that much effort and i did relatively fine

Now keep in mind, it's 216, so the class isn't already easy. Concepts are already harder than usual when compared to content from 131/132. So I wouldn't say it's really easy to get an A in 216 generally. Pros: - Teaches really well. Like really well. You will understand computer systems on a deeper level than any other professor teaching 216 in my opinion. - He loves teaching. That means he's not like one of those professors with really bad quality slides and unorganized course structure. The entire course is structured and organized really well under him, so you'll definitely enjoy 216 somewhat with him. - Pre-recorded lectures. That means no need to go to lecture and you should still do fine - Lots of extra credit opportunities. Basically go to his classes, participate, and get engagement points, which can help you get extra credits. Even by participating on Piazza by responding to other people's questions should give you engagement points as well. He also gives these weekly quizzes which is basically like a free grade to you since you get unlimited tries. - Open Notes exams. This is honestly the best part of the class. Take really good notes in my opinion, organize it all really well (I suggest using Obsidian notes since that's what I used to organize it all). This is also why his exams are generally harder than usual I believe. - No secret tests on projects. This is honestly the best part of the projects. It would be horrible if there were secret tests as it would take eons to debug them. Cons: - Hard projects. Yea I had trouble doing his projects at times since it's quite convoluted and wack and sometimes really long. Especially the Assembly project (all the best with that one). It's not bad as in impossible Yoon level bad, it's just quite challenging and you will be getting a lot of headaches by trying to figure out his projects, but they do generally help with understanding the material. - Hard exams. Compared to Nelson, Kauffman's exams are generally hard. So you will have to study and practice a lot for his exams, as well as his final. It's not like impossible hard, it's do able, but there aren't a lot of practice materials in my opinion so yeah you'll have to look at his recordings, projects, and other materials for practice and all. REMEMBER, study your projects really well, it's definitely almost always on all of his exams. Overall he's a great professor, but I suggest taking Nelson if you can (although he can have some cons too) as he provides more exam materials.

Overall a pretty good lecturer. Class is usually engaging, but sometimes he takes a lot of questions which can make the class feel a bit boring at times. I stopped going to discussions after the first few because they're optional, but you should go if you need help with the weekly labs. The weekly homeworks are easy to get 100% on since you can keep resubmitting each MCQ question until you get it right (although you should probably try to do it seriously). The projects are very lengthy, so make sure to start them ASAP. Exams are open note, but they are all conceptual questions, so you don't really look at your notes all that often. The exams are also pretty long and take almost the full 75 minutes allotted. There is a ton of opportunity for extra credit through completing all the labs (you only need to do 10 of them for full credit, but there are 14 total), asking questions during lecture, and answering piazza posts.
Took for 132/216 last year, Dr yoon would teach you how to ride a bike then expect you to know how to drive a car

I don't think I understood much from Kauffman's lectures. His slides and lecture code was often hard to follow and he information overloads on his project docs, slides, and lab/hmw directions. Although some may find it helpful with given information, I often had a hard time understanding what exactly I am supposed to do with the given information and how I can learn/complete the tasks. I found his projects to be mentally breaking, and I don't agree that this is what is best for students. I believe this class took a huge toll on my mental health and emotional stability. but I don't think Kauffman's 216 would be a great learning experience as you would have to struggle for basically nothing. Last but not least, I don't even understand why we had to learn Assembly in his class because I find Assembly to be completely useless and no one uses it anymore in modern programming. Take this class with another professor if you can

He's nice but a really bad teacher. His lectures make me almost fall asleep each class. He posts none of his practice tests, only other teachers' practice tests. The average for every exam was a falling grade while the other 216 classes had an A average on every exam. Randomly on a test I got hit with a question with a shebang when we've never covered anything like that.

I previously took the course, got an A plus, but Kauffman seems more concerned about people getting good grades than teaching the topic. I didn't retain everything in the class which doesn't make sense considering I didn't study for the exams and somehow got an A plus. Idk if there is a better professor for this class tho cuz all the professors seemed bad.

Extremely engaging lecturer and will make sure you remember concepts. Frequently reviews material and common misconceptions. Will not let anyone use their laptops during class, which is great since I always get distracted when nobody else pays attention and the vibe is distracted. Bad if you need to take notes on it, but honestly taking notes is useless in this class since A) he posts everything on the slides online B) he really makes sure you remember stuff by explaining things many times. His projects are actually fun and he ACTUALLY PROVIDES material on how to use grace and unix unlike some teachers. Every concept has a resource and detailed explanation online. He does password protect his project pdfs, which I found annoying at first but appreciated since it made me have to type out commands for a few seconds and thus learn what to do. Has graded assignments in class but based on completion and guaranteed free points as long as you show up. The assignments really help since he goes over it and its much more easy to remember than just listening for hours. Participation is also free points as long as you ask a question or answer a question.

It's all been said already, but he's amazing. However, don't mistake the high rating as meaning that he gives out free A's. If you want that, take Nelson. Kauffman challenges you to actually learn and because he's an excellent lecturer I was happy to do that. Felt like I learned a lot in this class and the work is fair if you put in the effort.

I want to start by saying that this course is hard. This was personally my first time having to go through learning about systems in depth and using some of the most barebone languages in coding. But Prof Kauffman is one of the best lecturers I have ever had. He is very understanding and communicated great with us students. Assignments such as quizzes and labs are the easiest grade boosts. The organization of the class with all of the labs, projects, and his websites were very useful. I rarely was engaged with lectures, but the recordings and slides were very detailed and easy to catch up with. In terms of the exams, I believe they were very fair. Along with being open note, they coincided heavily with his lecture examples and practice exam, but most with the labs and projects associated with the class. As long as you put in just a little effort for the things that matter like exams and projects, you should pass this class perfectly fine.

Professor Yoon is a laid-back guy, but not the best teacher. He tends to go off on long tangents that don't add much value and mostly teaches directly from the slides, which can feel pretty slow and unengaging. The practice exams and projects he provides don’t really align with the actual tests, which can be frustrating. On the bright side, the test averages are usually in the 60s, so there’s a generous curve. I ended with a 74% and got a B so if that's the kind of thing you're into this is the guy for you.

To whom it may concern, Yoon's exams can be challenging. To do well on them, make sure you really play around with edge cases on the compiler, modify the lecture example code. Do not think about this class as a class about C, just how bits and such are set. Assembly will inevitably feel hard, just practice and it should be ok. For projects, many of the hidden test cases are decently predictable, make sure your program can handle what the spec says it should handle. For example, if you are making a playlist program and the spec says the number of songs will never exceed 80, make sure you test how the program handles an empty playlist or a completely full one. They can really take a chunk out of your grade, but these hidden tests are usually just testing small edge cases. Make sure you check gradescope for the lab assignments as they aren't always announced. He sets cuttoffs at the end, I think they were lowered by around 3% for my specific scenario. He is a really nice guy.

I wasn’t a huge fan of this class overall. The projects were challenging, but they were also very effective in helping me understand the concepts covered in lectures and I liked that they were all public tests. My biggest tip for future students is to focus on fully understanding the projects and not just finishing them, as this will help a lot on exams. Many exam questions required implementing a function as an extension of a recent project, which I found a bit unfair. Projects are designed to take time and allow collaboration with TAs, so having similar tasks on timed exams felt unrealistic. On the other hand, the homeworks and labs were helpful for reinforcing concepts. However, one thing I didn’t like was having to complete them before those concepts were taught in lecture a lot of the time. This often made them feel pointless initially, and I ended up revisiting the labs and homeworks closer to exams to actually learn from them. What I struggled with most were the exams. Despite being open note, they were extremely difficult, and the single practice exam provided by Kauffman was significantly easier than the actual exams, making it less useful for preparation. Even though I reviewed all the labs, homeworks, projects, lecture slides, and attended or watched every lecture, I still found the exams to be tough.
Anyone who got below an A in this class was clearly either not trying or not paying any attention. He gives the answers to the exam during his lecture, you just have to listen.

Very solid professor, so much better than the other 216 professors. Kauffman is a great lecturer and makes concepts easy to understand, but whenever a student asks a question, he happens to go on a 5-15 minute tangent answering that question and makes lecture boring and hard to pay attention to. Despite this, his slides and lectures are really useful (he also records his lectures so it is not mandatory to go to lecture). Additionally, discussion depends on your TAs but im my opinion, it is useless. Most student havent gone since the first week or so of class and have made it out fine. My tas barely taught for like 5 mins and thats it. one thing to keep in mind is: START PROJECTS EARLY (AND DO NOT CHATGPT THEM). Kauffman tends to make very lengthy projects and even asks project related questions (even coding questions) on his exam, so make sure to lock in for those. Exams, on the other hand, weren't that bad tbh. Take your time and do not rush them because they will mostly take the whole time. Just because it is open note doesnt mean that you should not study at all because open note is not that useful (still useful tho).

Projects are the same as Nelsons but so are the practice tests which are NOTHING like the actual tests. The average for every test is low to mid 60s I stayed a few points above average for every test and made sure to get as many points as possible through labs, excercises, and projects. I've never had test anxiety before this class but this class made me get it because no matter how much you study the tests are awful. The amount of crashouts I had over exams is insane but looking back if you have to take this professor you will be ok as long as you focus on projects, labs, and excercises. Sometimes he posts labs without announcing it so if youre not in class you could miss it so make sure to stay on top of that. Yoon was nice when I had to makeup a quiz hes a good guy but if youre class is averaging 60s for exams its not every students fault, its the professors and he needs to realize that. When I took 216 the options were Nelson, Kauffman, Yoon but no spaces for Nelson or Kauffman were open bbut if I couldve picked based on my friends experiences I would pick Nelson.

Kauffman is an amazing CS professor. I would 100% recommend taking him. That being said, here is how his class works: Lectures are 75-minutes, he records everything, but it is still helpful to attend since he is a good lecturer and he takes a lot of questions during lecture. Each time you ask a question, it counts as extra credit. Discussions are pretty much free. We are required to complete at least 10 labs for the course (there are 12 labs in total). The TA will pretty much give the answers to the lab in discussion and you're allowed to collaborate with others on it. On top of that, close to project deadlines, discussions are converted to extra office hours which can be pretty helpful. It is really easy to get a 100% on every homework since it gives you the option to submit each question and the entire form. After every question, it tells you whether or not your answer was right, and you can keep resubmitting until you get all questions right. The projects are very long and difficult. Start early cause office hours (located on the 4th floor of A.V. Williams for some reason) fills up very fast and it can be either very hard or not possible to get help around the project deadline. In terms of difficulty, I found projects 1, 3, and 5 to be the most time consuming. Project 3 was both the most difficult and time consuming since it was the assembly project. The late penalty is very lenient, so there not much of problem with turning them in late. The exams are open note, but they can be quite hard. Rather than focusing on memorizing random things, they try to test big picture concepts and your ability to understand and apply them to sample programs given. There is no multiple choice, only short answer and coding questions. Furthermore, the exams can be a time crunch since almost all questions need a detailed response, so it's very easy to run out of time if you don't manage it on the exam. Despite how hard the projects and exams can be I still recommend taking Kauffman since he is a great teacher, a really nice and helpful person, and you will learn a lot of useful concepts from his class.

Great professor to take 216 with if you want to learn something. Good lecturer, fair exams (if you study and considering that they're open note), and extra credit on projects/exams/lectures. 216 is a hard class at Maryland but Kauffman is probably your best shot for learning. Take Nelson if you want an easy but less in depth class.

So much extra credit. Discussions are useless. Lectures are engaging. You can easily excel in the class even without touching the textbook. If you go to (or watch recordings of) all the lectures and complete all labs and homework on your own time honestly (not just copying or clicking through) it's a surprisingly easy A.

This class with this professor is designed to weed out students that are not serious about CS. I believe because I got an A- (I did not attend to any TA office hours or any office hours for this class because idk) there is definitely a very good possibility for you to understand and pass this class with yoon. I cannot defend his exams because it is a curveball, he thinks they're are fair in his eyes because he assumes you go to every lecture, understand everything, attend office hours, and attend every discussion.... but that's not reality. I am an average student and am not smart by any means, all I did was spend hours on the content he would teach, and spend time on it until it made sense and I can replicate his example codes (that's essentially what the projects are). The only way through this class is TIME and be WILLING to lose a good amount of sleep. Spend enough time on anything and you can learn anything in this class. That's a promise. Jesus loves you!

He's clearly a very knowledgeable professor. Additionally, he's an approachable and is more than happy to clarify concepts that you need clarification for. So overall, as a person and instructor he's great. The only gripe are how he gives assessments. He does not prepare you at all for exams, and he will sometimes add questions relating to one very esoteric or specific thing that he talked about once during lecture and not present in any lecture slides, lab assignments, and projects. Additionally, he'll sometimes give past exams or worksheets, but these also do not reflect the type of questions in exams. So, this is what makes his exams a lot more challenging. You will do fine in the class as long as you're not doing unrelated stuff on your laptop or phone like playing brawl stars or doom scrolling. Pay attention during lecture, take and REVIEW notes, and you will be fine. I believe he gives a reasonable curve at the end, I think a 75 is a B.

If you are reading this to decide what professor to take and you are early enough not to pick Yoon, dont. However, if you have to take him do not spend the months leading up to the class worrying like I did. Yoon’s class is not equivalent to all of these reviews and you need to go in which a much more positive mindset than these reviews let on. It is definitely true that my friends in Nelson and Kauffman had an easier time and arguably learned more, however having Yoon does not mean that you are going to fail. I am no where near a genius, in fact I was just hoping to pass 216 knowing how hard would be, especially with Yoon and I ended with a B. What is takes to do well in Yoon’s course is hard work. His exams are impossible and not fair. The test material does not reflect what’s taught in class and labs so I would say that doing bad on exams is expected (class average was never higher than a 66). Quizzes also very hard. The projects are doable, start early. The lab assignments are free As if you attend lab. You need to try very hard to do well on projects and lab assignments because these free points will average out exam grades. More importantly, you need to take advantage of Yoon’s willingness to help. Ive seen other reviews say this and it is so true.. Yoon is truly SUCH a nice guy. I had a family emergency and Yoon granted me an extension and offered to help fix my code without even considering it for a second. I went to Yoon’s personal office hours multiple time throughout the semester to ask questions from lecture or go over old exam problems, and I learned a ton. You will excel if you build a relationship with Yoon, and I suggest starting early!! The class was averaging low scores, so towards the end of the semester Yoon assigned an easy extra credit assignment that would add points to your final grade and yes, Yoon curved our final grades. In conclusion, you can absolutely do well in this class without being a genius, Yoon just does not make it easy. You need to attend every lab and lecture, earning as many free points as you can and studying enough to stay with the average exam score. Most importantly, review lecture slides and if you are ever confused (or just want Yoon to know who you are), GO SEE HIM!!!

If you are reading this in order to decide what professor to take and you are early enough to not pick Yoon, don't. However, do not stress for the months leading up to the course if you have to take him like I did. His course is not equivalent to all of these reviews and you should go in with a more positive mindset than these reviews let on. I am not a genius, in fact I was just hoping to pass 216 knowing how hard it would be, especially with Yoon. Without a doubt, my friends in Nelson and Kauffman had a much easier time and arguably learned more. However, Yoon does not mean you are failing the class, I ended with a B. What it takes to do well in this class is hard work. His exams are impossible and not fair. They do not resemble the material taught in class, so doing bad on the exams I would say is expected (class average was no higher than a 66). The projects are totally doable, start early. The lab assignments are also free As if you attend labs. I did not miss a single lecture or lab. I tried very hard on projects and lab assignments trying to get as many "free" points as I could which will balance out the exam grades. What it comes down to as well is that Yoon is truly such a nice guy. I had an emergency and he granted me an extension without even considering it. You have to take advantage of his willingness to help. I went to Yoon's personal office hours multiple times to ask questions from lectures or go over exam questions. During these sessions I learned a ton. You should try very hard to build a relationship with Yoon and start this early in the semester. While the class was averaging low scores, he gave us an easy extra credit assignment towards the end of the semester and yes at the end of the course, he did curve our final grades. In conclusion, I wouldnt be eager to take his classes and he makes it very difficult but trying to get as many points as you can and studying enough to stay with the average on exams will get you very far. Most importantly, review the lecture slides after class and if you are confused GO SEE HIM!!!

Very good lecturer and recordings were provided. Projects forced you to learn the content and were not too difficult. I really enjoyed how the exams were open-note because it made you rely on your understanding of the content and projects rather than just memorization. Probably the best professor ive had at umd yet.

just terrible. please do not take if at all possible. his exams are really unfair relative to other professors since he puts no effort into providing his own practice material which would actually help on his midterms and quizzes, and he is actually the worst lecturer I've ever witnessed. he's nice and that's the only good thing about his class. just pray for the curve and make sure you do well on all the projects, especially at the end because he'll assign the final project on finals week.

I personally was not a big fan of this class. Kauffman is a great lecturer and makes concepts easy to understand, but there were some elements of the class that I was not a big fan of. I found difficulty in completing many of the projects, and the TAs were not very helpful in my opinion. The exams are open note, which while appealing to many, made them much more difficult to me. A lot of the questions on the exam were not based on the topics discussed in lecture or even projects, but tested a much more deep understanding of the C programming language. I found the exams to be very challenging and the grading for them to be very harsh as well.

Kauffman is the GOAT. Best teaching style I have ever come across. 216 is a hard class but he made the concepts very understandable. All exams are open note which is helpful at times but does not help that much, you still have to know what you are doing. My only issue with him is that I think the projects are very very difficult, sometimes too difficult imo. The projects do help you prepare for the exams tho. Overall very good prof, very good speaker, very good at breaking down hard cs concepts. Although I got a worse grade in 216 then 132 I feel like I understand the course material way better. Ending note Kaufman is the GOAT and an absolute gift to the CS department(Just please make the projects easier)

No professor is perfect but Prof K comes as close to that as one can expect. His lectures are well planned and the course material is as well. The labs are well planned out and give you an introduction to the material. Make sure do them yourself and understand what going on same. Same thing can be said with the weekly homeworks that have quizzes (you can retake until you get a 100%) but make sure to understand what you are doing. Make sure to understand the code pack as it's impossible to go through everything in lecture and fully understand it. His projects are the hardest I had so far in CS here at UMD. The take hours to complete and much more complicated and intricate than projects you have done in 131/132. Though there are only 5 of them, each take hours to complete which each becoming progressively harder, but not neccesarily longer. While in 131/132 you could do projects that day off or a night before you CAN'T do that here. His projects take a minimum of 10-15 hours and the assembly one took me upwards of 25-30 hours to complete. Furthermore his exams are not as easy as the appear. The practice material is standardized and very meek with some resemblance but little to none. Exam 1 I found was hard but Exam 2 was easier though assembly is a much harder concept. The open resource policy can be deceiving but Kauffman does not ask theory questions on his exams as much, given the open resource. Make sure to plan resourceful notes that have tons of examples regarding coding and debugging and analyzing issues. Kauffman belief is that you should have access to your laptop and is a very takes an industry approach to the class. His grading structure put an heavy emphasis on exams each being worth 20% and the final the same (60% total). Other professors had much laxer exam percentages and focused on projects and lab exercises more. His labs quizzes are grade boosters as you can a 100%. I would have wished for a 3rd exam and each exam being worth like 12-15% and the projects being worth more as I spent hours on them. TLDR: Harder teacher than most, if you want an easier teacher who's funny take Nelson but if you really want to understand Operating Systems take Kauffman.

Kauffman is the goat!! I'd highly recommend him for 216! It's a difficult class but he's a good lecturer & has a fun personality. Lots of opportunities for extra credit (not just from participating in lecture). Discussions are not required, I did not go to a single one. Exams are open note, open lecture slides, open past coding projects, etc. Exams are graded leniently too. No multiple choice on exams (which I consider a good thing). There are 5 projects over the course of the semester. This is a lot fewer than in 131/132, but they are much more challenging. That said, the projects felt very applicable to real life and it felt like you'd gained useful skills once you completed one (except for the Assembly one maybe). Start the projects early. Office hours are packed, use Piazza instead and read other people's posts to avoid issues before you encounter them.

Solid professor. His lectures are reasonably interesting and they explain the content well. His discussions are honestly completely useless but they operate as extra office hours later in the semester so if you need extra help with a project or want greater understanding of a lab you can go. He gives labs & "quizzes" which serve as grade boosters and learning checks (I say "quizzes" because you can retake until 100%) His exams are very fair and are open note. This forces you to prepare notes for the exam because you will need to reference them at times but by creating notes you are also studying so I like the format. The slides have alot of info and all lectures are recorded so you could not show up if you want (i did but you do you). Overall solid teacher, recorded lectures, optional discussion, fair open note exams.

Pros: - Pretty nice guy. He tends to give relatively reasonable accommodations to students - Records lectures Cons: - Very bad at explaining certain topics without either getting sidetracked and talking about something completely random, or speaking about something conceptually difficult almost as though we learned about it already - Does not relate difficult topics to things we have learned already - Very unfair exam questions. Many trick questions and content unrelated to what was on the practice tests - Gives assignments to complete during finals week, including a project due after finals (assigned late) - Pretty heavy accent, so it's hard to understand what he is saying sometimes. Overall, not my cup of tea for a professor.
I don't even know what else to say besides this guy is amazing and he cares so much about his students. I urge you to take him for anything and I'm praying I can take a future class with him.
Bruh he the goat. Amazing lecturer, great at explaining complex issues. Thorough projects that really teach you the material. Fair open note exams, ways to earn bonus points, he does it all.

Truly the goat. He knows what every student wants and does it. This class is made easier in a perfect way without sacrificing learning the material. If you have the option to take this man's class for anything take it. You won't regret it.
If you have to choose between Yoon and delaying taking 216, take the extra semester, your GPA will thank you.
Leaving this to offset the one guy who reviewed him one star and instead recommended Herman. Dont take herman

Probably the best professor I will ever have, clearly cares about his students, wants to see everyone succeed, exams are fair, projects are hard but when done will help a lot in the exams. Currently the only professor whos making 216 fun and single handedly increasing the GPA of this course.

tbh i didnt even take this guy but my roommate did. I can't even focus on my assignments because every night my roommate is crashing out and banging on his table because of some project yoon gave. i thought it would be over by finals week but here we are with yoon assigning a final project and my roommate at the point of being assigned to a mental ward.

First of all, this professor's lectures do not help to clarify or convey important information needed for projects and exams. Often times, his lectures start off with the concept but devolve into other unimportant topics that do not apply to projects. I find myself learning the material on my own. Second, his exams are not fair. As in, they test less on your general understanding of the subject, and more on "edge cases" or scenarios that the average student is unlikely to run into during their time with the programming language. Both the first and second midterms for this class had an average score of 66/100. With the average of the class just on the edge of a C- Students who pay attention to the class and students who get a good exam grade are not correlated for this professor in any way. Avoid him if you can. If you do HAVE to take him (due to sections for other professors being filled up), try your best to learn on your own and hopefully with a good TA. It will be the only way to make it through his course with a good grade.

so many trolls. should be a 1-2 star average tbh. He assigned a lab on december 9th and its due december 10th keep in mind finals start on the 11th. also we have a project due on the 18th. not sure if you guys know but the last day of the semester is the 17th. so i do wonder what goes in his head. also the average is kinda awful but we ball tho. i hope he curves at the end.

Absolutely the best professor I've had at UMD. Lectures were so much fun and I never wanted to skip. He's a very helpful and supportive professor who 100% cares about his students and wants them to succeed. Definitely a difficult class but you learn so much.

Amazing professor. Engaging lectures and fair point system. He loves answering questions, and encourages students to come to his office hours to learn more. There are only 5 projects and all were manageable. Exams were also fair. 6/5 stars.

One of the best professors I've ever had. The lectures are super engaging and he does a good job of clearly explaining the content. As many other reviews have said, this class is definitely not easy, I would start the projects early and keep up to date with the lectures. If you do all the projects and labs, you should be prepared for the midterms and final and will pretty much be guaranteed a good grade. Take him if you have the chance to.

I beg you to not take his class. Without his burner accounts leaving 5 star reviews he will have a 1.00 rating. I am only leaving 1 star because you cannot rate 0. The class average is a 71% right now two weeks before finals. Yoon causes people to switch out of cs. Do not take Yoon.

so goated definitely take him for 216 if you can! make sure that you put effort into this class because its not easy AND START THE PROJECTS EARLY but professor kauffman is genuinely the goat the class is organized so well & he cares about his students and i would absolutely recommend him

Kauffman is great. This is not an easy class; however, he provides so many resources (lecture recordings, recommendations for textbooks, answers to our HWs and labs before the exam, active on Piazza, office hours, etc.) I would recommended taking 216 with Kauffman. Lecture is not mandatory but you have the option for "extra credit" if you participate in lecture. He is also a kind guy :)

Very generous and kind professor who cares about his students and what they learn. The material in 216 is somewhat difficult but Kauffman does his best to simplify it and make it easy to digest. He is very lenient with students, willing to push due dates and deadlines back when necessary. His tests are open note but not necessarily easy, so while notes are helpful it's important that you understand core concepts. Projects get progressively more difficult as the semester goes on but there are plenty of resources from office hours almost every day to relevant piazza posts. Kauffman is always in a happy mood and keeps class very engaging and offers extra credit when you participate in class. Overall an amazing professor and I would recommend taking his class.

Take Kauffman. 100% will not regret it. pros: - cares about his students, and has great resources and clarity on the subject. - lots of scope to boost grade with labs, hw, and extra credit - only 5 projects and have only public tests - exams are very fair, do the projects to succeed in the exams cons: - projects may be challenging, but keep up with the labs and go to discussion and you will be able to do them easily

Super nice guy, but spends about 1/3 of lecture time going on tangents or joking with students. I just stopped going to class after the first week (he posts recordings) and watched the recordings on 1.5x. Exams were almost always copy pasted from one of the previous exams (which he gives you) and felt absurdly easy compared to other classes I've taken. Projects were also very fair, just start early and the workload isn't bad. I would highly recommend taking Nelson for 216 if you are able.

One of the worse professors i have ever had. I dont know why people like him the projects r too isnaen and the tests are not easy bcaue they are open note. this guy sucks dont take, take herman instead

Kauffman is the ultimate professor. Not only is he a fantastic lecturer, but his policies make succeeding in his course super achievable! Projects are public tests only, exams are open note (and you can write/test code during them!), and he has an insane amount of opportunities to receive extra-credit. He’s awesome, and I really learned a lot from his class!
Not a good professor!!!!!!!!!!!! GOD AWFUL DO NOT TAKE HIM IF YOU WANT TO NOT CRASH OUT!!!! These 5 star reviews are all fake don't trust! Anyways if you have him good luck because hes not good.

Very messy professor, he will gives you deadline after deadline as if his class is the only class that matters, as if you are not studying any other classes. His projects and labs are the only fair part of his class. He is nice and calm, but gives the worse tests ever, why does other class gets to have open notes and simpler questions but we have to had extra hard questions and closed notes .His practice test might as well be useless since it has the most basic problems only for the quiz and exam to asks you for the tiniest, specific details. I have had many CS teachers before, but Mr. Yoon's class makes my mental health drops to an alarming rate. If you value your mental health, do not take this teacher!

Heed all the advice everybody has put here. I would rather not take 216 than have him. Gladly next semester he is not teaching it. I self studied so much and all of his practice tests were truly useless and not similar to the actual to the exam in any way. Our recent average was a 66/103 and NO CURVE. I think that says a lot about his teaching and his tests. His projects are also not too bad but they take so much time and there are so many edge cases that you won’t be aware of until you get it back. Overall, i really disliked taking him this semester
Professor Yoon is such a sweet guy and is trying his best. He is a fantastic lecturer and his exams are fair! :)
Yoon must of been taught by Benjamin Netanyahu and Kim Jun Un.

Great teacher that only wants the best for his students. Content can be tricky but a lot of his resources can help you succeed. Just make sure you do well throughout the class as his final can be tricky. Overall great teacher

didnt go to a single lecture, didnt watch a single recording, spammed the practice exam 2 days before each exam, got a B. My only complaint is that the final didn't have a practice and also the assembly stuff was kinda annoying. not even any real complaints

I took Nelson over the summer of 2024, and to be honest, you'll pass if you study his previous exams and you submit your projects on time. You can learn off the slides but personally I didn't get much help from them. A lenient grader (except the final) and he's very anal about academic integrity. If you're having trouble balancing things or struggling with grades, talk to him, he's very understanding. Also he has a fun personally so he likes to joke and laugh a lot. If you're going to take 216, take it with Nelson.

I think Nelson is hands-down the best introductory computer-science teacher in the department. He does a great job at welcoming people, motivating people, making the class engaging with his students, cares for each and every student above and beyond, etc. He is very open to receiving feedback and input into how he runs the class. Very honorable as well, as he doesn't tolerate cheating and gives students the grade they deserve in the class, regardless of his opinion of a certain student. His tests are extremely fair and what he covers in lecture will be on the tests. Class is extremely relatable to the tests and he is super knowledgeable about programming and the real world of computer science. He promotes fairness, equality, and hard work while going out of his way to help people form groups within and outside of the class, so people feel connected in a world of disconnected computer-scientists. 100% Take him if you get the chance.

Quite literally the goat in almost every way possible. I had a FAR easier time in this class compared to Herman's 132 (not that Herman is bad, I personally love him but he definitely runs a tough class.) Provides far more exam practice then you will ever need and the content is very easy to manage, especially if you take it in the summer like I did. Exam 3 was almost the exact same as the practice given, so as long as you spend 5 or so hours practicing per exam you should do good. He's also incredibly nice and approachable, and apart from sometimes getting sidetracked in lectures there really isn't anything bad I can say about him.

In terms of difficulty, Nelson's 216 is a joke. Provides 10+ years of practice exams and all midterms are basically recycled, to the point where content on these exams honestly felt unfairly easy. Projects felt pretty fair, as long as you space your work out there shouldn't really be a problem. My biggest problem with Nelson was his lectures, it felt like he often got sidetracked and I stopped going in person after a week because I felt like I was getting nothing out of class. What helped a lot was his past recorded videos, these are much more concise and cut out all of the tangents from his in person lectures.
CMSC216 with this professor was easy not hard at all

Lowkey kind of hard and fast pace but Nelson is very kind and will offers a lot of support and help. Would definitely reccommend taking this over the summer if you have a lot going on during the school year. I also would not reccommend doing 216 and 250 together. Take 216 over the summer if you can.

Absolutely take 216 (or any other class for that matter) with Kauffman if you have a chance. First of all, there are a lot of perks class-wise: no secret or release tests, all exams are open note, bonus points for doing discussion exercises, quizzes had unlimited attempts, etc. He also is just a very kind professor who seems to care a lot about how his students do. I went to his office hours and he spent several hours going through my code with me. My one issue in this class was that I did find projects very difficult, but I feel like that would be the case with any professor and I also think that I struggled with them more than most of the other students did.

TL;DR : Kauffman was an incredibly good prof, and I have almost no complaints. His class was a little tough, but anyone looking to take 216 should definitely take this prof. Pros: - He's a pretty chill guy and he just has a good energy to him. Lectures are pretty enjoyable, and he answers questions very well - Each of his discussion sections have 3-4 TA's so there's lots of opportunities to get extra help if you need it. - There's no secret tests for his projects, so you'll pretty much always know what parts of the code you're failing - Lectures are recorded - He gives extra credit for engagement in class lectures and discussions, and answering questions in piazza - He gives optional makeup credit for projects, so if you lose points on a project, you can make them up in the future or vice versa - His exams are open note, so you don't really need to spend time memorizing all of the small stuff Cons: - The projects got to be pretty tough near the end of the semester. While they started off fair and balanced, as the semester when on, the project instructions started to become more and more unclear, and this would lead to errors that wouldn't really be the students' faults. The test cases for the projects also because more sparse and the semester went on. - During lecture, you can get extra credit for asking questions, but a decent amount of the time, questions just ended up being not realted or very loosley related to the content, and so many people were asking questions that the lectures could feel very broken up at times. For 10 minutes you would be learning stuff, and then the next 10 minutes would be questions that aren't helpful for the majority of the class (at least, in my opinion). It would just kind of be a cycle like that, so it's kind of difficult to keep focus in lectures - This isn't really Kauffman's fault, but office hours would be packed near project deadlines. While this sould partially be expected from students, the queue would end up being 12-15 students long near project deadlines, so you would be better off just going to a friend for help.

Amazing professor. He really cares about his students and wants them to succeed. Exams are straightforward and open note (averages were pretty high). There are weekly labs and hw, but they only take like 1 or 2 hours tops. Projects are really time consuming. I got away with doing the project the night before they was due in Fawzi's 132 but could not do that for this class. Some of the project descriptions are confusing, but if you start earlyish you should be fine. There were 5 projects and no secret test for any of the projects. He also offers a lot of extra credit (lecture participation and extra stuff on projects). I thought project 3 was the hardest - mostly because of the puzzlebin part. Would definitely recommend prof k!!

my goat, only got a b+ cuz of my own fuck ups, probably the best professor ive ever had at umd, highly recommend for 216 projects are moderately difficult, exams are butt easy, and gives free points for labs and hws, late policy is very fair as well

Nelson is a pretty good professor and a nice guy. He likes to joke around in lectures and can waste a lot of time when he gets distracted, but he (usually) gets through the content he needs to cover and will always provide recordings to cover what he doesn't get to in lecture. Some people find him getting off-topic distracting, but it actually helped me pay attention since I also get distracted often and his jokes are a nice brain break. Nelson's an active poster on the course Piazza and is extremely transparent about things like grading and exam content. Exams are extremely reasonable and Nelson provides plenty of practice materials and is extremely clear on what they will cover. Projects/exercises aren't unreasonable. For reference, I found the projects to be less convoluted than projects from Fawzi's CMSC 132, but the instructions are more confusing and unclear. Ultimately they aren't terribly difficult, just start early and ask him clarifying questions if you get confused.

Nelson Definitely Made 216 a lot easier than I thought it was going to be. The class is grade is comprised of 36% Project Grade, 4% Quizzes, and 46% Exams, and 14% Final Exam. Originally I thought the Exams were going to be the most difficult part of the course, but the averages on them were all 90% or above if I recall correctly. The projects took a long time usually but doable in like 3 days, I always made sure to start them early and always ended with at least 2 days of extra time before the project was due.
